title: Contemplating Real-World Object Classification

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Real-world applications frequently provide challenges that are not seen in common computer vision datasets like Imagenet, where images are blurry, dark, corrupted, objects are highly occluded, test objects may be out of distribution due to natural distribution shifts, etc.  This phenomenon was investigated in 2018 by Beery et al, who similarly found categorization is easier with localization in challenging real-world scenes (ie classifying cropped boxes). I would recommend taking a look at that paper (citation below) and including it in the related work.

Beery, S., Van Horn, G., & Perona, P. (2018). Recognition in terra incognita. In Proceedings of the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV) (pp. 456-473).


Pros:

The authors provide many experiments digging into various aspects of what makes real-world object detection challenging. This is a useful reference point for future work.

Cons:
This paper re-analyzes generalization in the context of an existing dataset. There is not anything particularly novel about the analysis, and similar results have been shown on other real-world datasets.
The authors describe the image distortions they consider to be “natural”, but are applying them synthetically. It is not clear to me that applying a synthetic distortion of a type that can be seen in the real world is necessarily reflective of those realistic distortions in the wild.  It would be better to explicitly collect examples of these types of distortions in real data and compare against that.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:
Real-world applications frequently provide challenges that are not seen in common computer vision datasets like Imagenet, where images are blurry, dark, corrupted, objects are highly occluded, test objects may be out of distribution due to natural distribution shifts, etc. The manuscript effectively highlights these crucial discrepancies between benchmark performance and real-world generalization, particularly focusing on the ObjectNet dataset. This phenomenon, where categorization is easier with localization in challenging real-world scenes (i.e., classifying cropped boxes), was similarly investigated in 2018 by Beery et al., who explored recognition in "terra incognita" scenarios. I would recommend taking a look at that paper (Beery, S., Van Horn, G., & Perona, P. (2018). Recognition in terra incognita. In Proceedings of the European Conference on Computer Vision (ECCV) (pp. 456-473)) and including it in the related work section, as it aligns well with the paper's core premise regarding the benefits of isolating objects from complex backgrounds. The paper's re-analysis of ObjectNet, a dataset specifically designed to reduce inherited biases from ImageNet, underscores the ongoing struggle of deep models to generalize beyond their training distribution, even with significant performance gains when isolating objects.

Pros:

The authors provide many experiments digging into various aspects of what makes real-world object recognition challenging, making this a useful reference point for future work. The systematic approach to re-evaluating deep models on ObjectNet is commendable. Specifically, the paper thoroughly investigates how widely-used deep models like AlexNet, VGG-19, and ResNet-152 perform when applied to isolated objects (bounding boxes or segmentation masks) versus entire cluttered scenes, as detailed in Section 3.1 and illustrated in Figure 2. The finding that applying models to the object bounding box instead of the entire scene results in around a 20-30% performance improvement (using the authors' code for fair comparison) is a significant empirical contribution, recovering a notable portion of the dramatic performance loss originally reported by Barbu et al. (2019). The analysis extends beyond natural distribution shifts to include comprehensive robustness evaluations against synthetic image perturbations. This includes common image corruptions (e.g., Gaussian noise, impulse noise, various blurs, snow, frost, fog, as discussed in Section 3.2.1 and shown in Figures 37-38 in Appendix F) and adversarial attacks (FGSM and PGD-5, presented in Section 3.2.2 and Table 1). The consistent finding that limiting the object area to bounding boxes or segmentation masks leads to higher accuracy and robustness across these diverse perturbations (Figures 3, 4, 5, and 6) offers valuable insights into the role of context and foreground-background separation in model robustness, suggesting that background subtraction could be an effective adversarial defense. Furthermore, the qualitative analysis in Section 3.4, which categorizes reasons for human and model difficulty (e.g., objects in motion, occluded objects, wrong labels, difficult-to-recognize objects even for humans), coupled with illustrative examples in Figure 7 and Appendices C and D, provides a rich understanding of ObjectNet's inherent challenges. The discussion in Section 4 also thoughtfully outlines promising future research directions, such as further investigating background subtraction as a defense mechanism and exploring multi-label prediction accuracy as a more suitable evaluation metric for complex scenes.

Cons:
This paper re-analyzes generalization in the context of an existing dataset (ObjectNet), which, despite its merits, means there is not anything particularly novel about the fundamental analysis of deep model generalization failures. Similar results regarding the poor generalization of CNNs to out-of-distribution samples have been shown on other real-world datasets and benchmarks, such as ImageNetV2 (Recht et al., 2019) or WILDS (Koh et al., 2020), as acknowledged in the paper's related work (Section 1 and 2). While the paper provides a valuable re-evaluation of ObjectNet's specific characteristics and the impact of object isolation, particularly highlighting the "object detection" vs. "object recognition" confusion in the original ObjectNet paper, the core conclusion that deep models struggle with distribution shifts is a well-established observation in the field. The empirical findings, while robust and well-supported, primarily reinforce existing understandings about the limitations of current deep learning architectures rather than introducing a new conceptual framework for understanding these failures.

Additionally, the authors describe the image distortions they consider to be “natural” (e.g., Gaussian noise, various blurs, snow, frost, fog) but are applying them synthetically (Section 3.2.1). It is not clear to me that applying a synthetic distortion of a type that can be seen in the real world is necessarily reflective of those realistic distortions "in the wild." While these synthetic corruptions are standard benchmarks in robustness research (e.g., following Hendrycks & Dietterich (2019)), their controlled generation might not fully capture the complex, spatially varying, and often correlated noise patterns that arise organically in real-world scenarios. For instance, real-world snow or fog involves intricate physical interactions with light and objects that a simple overlay or filter might not replicate accurately, potentially leading to an incomplete assessment of true robustness to natural phenomena. It would be better to explicitly collect examples of these types of distortions from real data and compare against that, rather than relying solely on synthetically generated versions, to provide a more authentic measure of robustness to natural distribution shifts. The paper itself notes that "robustness measures for synthetic distribution shifts are weakly predictive of robustness on the natural distribution shifts" (Taori et al., 2020, cited in Section 2), which reinforces this concern regarding the generalizability of synthetic corruption findings to truly natural settings.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper revisits ObjectNet dataset closely and found applying classifiers on object bounding box significantly reduces the gap between ImageNet and ObjectNet. The authors further investigates the robustness of CNNs against image perturbations and adversarial attacks, and found limiting the object area to their segmentation mask significantly improves model accuracy (and robustness). Qualitative evaluation is also performed over confident and less-confident / incorrect model predictions and find it correlates with human perception.

Pros:
- More analyses like this paper does would help bridge the gap between ML/CV model performance in staged datasets and real-world scenarios
- The experimentation conducted in this paper is comprehensive, accompanied with many in-depth inspection over a large dataset. The insights drawn from this paper would be invaluable for researchers working in this field.

Cons:
- My major concern with this paper (and the main factor of rating it clear rejection) is the experimental setup used in section 3.3. From authors they "selected images from ten classes of the ObjectNet dataset ... manually annotated the object of interest in each image". Then "Models were trained on 70 images per category".  (also from Figure 39 "In total we annotated 1K images across ten categories of the ObjectNet dataset."). If interpreted correctly, the models are trained on part of ObjectNet images which clearly violates dataset license "ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model." (https://objectnet.dev/download.html). 

- While appreciating the authors conducting study on model robustness, the conclusion drawn from several experiments seems to confuse "performance" v.s. "robustness", where the former indicates model have better accuracy, and the latter measures how model accuracy varies with increasing noise / perturbations. See more details below.

Some (minor) comments:

- Sec 3.1. 1) I share the concern from authors that "object detector" should be not confused with "object recognition" (or commonly used "image classifier"). Hopefully vision community could use more consistent terms across literatures.

- Sec 3.1 1) While detection dataset would surely have more scale variation (and truncation / occlusion due to many objects are not in the center), it is not entirely clear that object in detection datasets "vary more in parameters such as lighting, ..., and blur".

- Sec 3.1 1) It would be great to see more analysis on detection datasets (authors mentioned they will discuss in section 4, but only with very little analysis).

- Sec 3.1, 2) While ImageNet and ObjectNet have distinct characteristics, having some stats on object size / spatial placement might better illustrate the gaps between these datasets.

- Sec 3.1 3) Agree top-5 might make classifiers' life easy, but it is more of an eval metric rather than training loss (the model still need to predict top-1 class correct during training). Meanwhile, it is not very clear why multi-label annotation would bias against model that "is spatially more precise but misses some objects". That model should be evaluated against detection benchmarks, rather than object recognition (image classification) datasets.

- Sec 3.1 "bounding box annotation". For multiple objects nearby with the same category, the annotation would include all of them in one bounding box. This might leads to bad aspect ratio? (in general, bounding box would also vary more in aspect ratio than images, and feeding bounding box into a square CNN seems to be less ideal)

- Sec 3.1 "object recognition results". "AlexNet, VGG-19, and GoogLeNet have also been used", GoogLeNet should be "ResNet-152"? ObjectNet uses inception (GoogLeNet) v4 while authors use v3.

- Sec 3.2.1 "The higher the prediction accuracy, the higher the robustness against natural distortions". This is not necessarily true. Looking at Figure 3, it seems all models and both image / bounding box schemes would have decayed performance w.r.t distortion severity, and their slopes seem similar.

- Sec 3.3 "Despite a large body of literature on whether and how much visual context benefits CNNs, the majority being focused on model accuracy, the matter has not been settled yet". "the matter" means context for robustness? please clarify.

- Sec 3.3 " Around 35.5% and 12.6% of the image pixels fall inside the object bounding box and the foreground object, respectively. Around 58.5% of the bounding box pixels fall inside the object mask.". here foreground object and object mask should be the same thing? if so, the numbers seem not matching (would expect box-to-image ratio * mask-to-box-ratio = mask-to-image ratio, or these numbers are normalized differently)?

- Figure 5: it is clear that seg-mask actually isn't robust to adversarial attacks (accuracy dropped significantly), which contradicts with the claim from authors.

- Sec 3.3 "An attempt was made to tune the parameters to attain the best test accuracy in each case", could authors elaborate? 

- Sec 3.3.1. here the results seem to indicate segmask is more robust (less variations). Would the segmentation mask itself be indicative for object categories? It might be interesting to predict object classes directly from those masks as a baseline.


Final review:

The authors updated the manuscript and removed tuning experiment on ObjectNet. I am still a bit concerned about the definition of "robustness", but the paper overall does look good for ICLR publication.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:
This paper revisits the ObjectNet dataset closely, aiming to re-evaluate the performance of deep object recognition models under real-world distribution shifts. The authors found that applying classifiers on object bounding boxes significantly reduces the performance gap observed between ImageNet and ObjectNet, suggesting that scene clutter and context play a larger role than initially assumed. The study further investigates the robustness of CNNs against various image perturbations, including common corruptions and adversarial attacks. A key finding from this analysis is that limiting the object area to their segmentation mask significantly improves both model accuracy and robustness across these synthetic challenges. Finally, a qualitative evaluation is performed over confident and less-confident/incorrect model predictions, revealing a correlation with human perception of object difficulty.

Pros:
- More analyses like this paper does would help bridge the gap between ML/CV model performance in staged datasets and real-world scenarios. The comprehensive investigation into how factors like contextual information (bounding boxes vs. full scenes) and segmentation masks affect model performance and robustness is crucial for understanding the limitations of current deep learning models. This work contributes significantly to the ongoing discourse on out-of-distribution generalization, moving beyond traditional ImageNet variations to a more challenging real-world testbed like ObjectNet.
- The experimentation conducted in this paper is comprehensive, accompanied with many in-depth inspection over a large dataset. The authors systematically evaluate six widely-used state-of-the-art deep neural networks, including AlexNet, VGG-19, and ResNet-152, across different input modalities (full image, bounding box, segmentation mask) and various types of perturbations (geometric, natural distortions, and adversarial attacks like FGSM and PGD-5). This rigorous approach, detailed across Sections 3.1, 3.2.1, 3.2.2, and 3.3.1, provides a multi-faceted view of model behavior. The insights drawn from this paper would be invaluable for researchers working in this field, particularly those focused on improving the generalization and robustness of computer vision models.

Cons:
- My major concern with this paper (and the main factor of rating it clear rejection) is the experimental setup used in section 3.3. From authors they "selected images from ten classes of the ObjectNet dataset ... manually annotated the object of interest in each image". Then "Models were trained on 70 images per category". (also from Figure 39 "In total we annotated 1K images across ten categories of the ObjectNet dataset."). If interpreted correctly, the models are trained on part of ObjectNet images which clearly violates dataset license "ObjectNet may never be used to tune the parameters of any model." (https://objectnet.dev/download.html). This license restriction is fundamental to ObjectNet's design as a purely held-out test set, intended to evaluate generalization without allowing models to adapt to its specific biases. Training or fine-tuning on any subset of ObjectNet, even for specialized experiments, undermines its purpose and could lead to an overestimation of model generalization ability, making the reported robustness gains questionable in this specific context.
- While appreciating the authors conducting study on model robustness, the conclusion drawn from several experiments seems to confuse "performance" v.s. "robustness", where the former indicates model have better accuracy, and the latter measures how model accuracy varies with increasing noise / perturbations. For example, in Section 3.2.1, the discussion around Figure 3 implies that higher prediction accuracy inherently leads to higher robustness, stating, "Overall, we conclude that limiting the object area only to the bounding box leads not only to higher prediction accuracy but also to higher robustness against image distortions." While these two aspects can be correlated, robustness specifically refers to the *degradation* rate under increasing perturbations, not just the absolute accuracy score. A model could have higher baseline accuracy but still be highly susceptible to noise, exhibiting a sharp drop in performance, which would indicate poor robustness. The paper would benefit from a clearer distinction and analysis of these metrics, especially when discussing the impact of input types (full image vs. bounding box vs. segmentation mask) on model resilience.

Some (minor) comments:

- Sec 3.1. 1) I share the concern from authors that "object detector" should be not confused with "object recognition" (or commonly used "image classifier"). Hopefully vision community could use more consistent terms across literatures. The current ambiguity can lead to misinterpretations of research findings and hinder direct comparisons between different tasks and model capabilities.
- Sec 3.1 1) While detection dataset would surely have more scale variation (and truncation / occlusion due to many objects are not in the center), it is not entirely clear that object in detection datasets "vary more in parameters such as lighting, ..., and blur". While detection datasets like MS COCO offer greater diversity in object presence and location, the claim about inherent variation in imaging conditions like lighting and blur compared to recognition datasets would require empirical evidence or specific dataset characteristics to fully substantiate.
- Sec 3.1 1) It would be great to see more analysis on detection datasets (authors mentioned they will discuss in section 4, but only with very little analysis). The discussion in Section 4, specifically the proposed future work to "see how well the state of the art object detectors perform on the ObjectNet dataset," highlights the potential value of such an analysis, but the paper does not extensively pursue it, limiting the scope of the current findings.
- Sec 3.1, 2) While ImageNet and ObjectNet have distinct characteristics, having some stats on object size / spatial placement might better illustrate the gaps between these datasets. Quantifying these differences, perhaps by comparing distributions of bounding box sizes or object centroids, could provide stronger empirical support for the claim that ImageNet-trained models struggle due to differences in object presentation. Figure 41 (Appendix H) does provide object scale distribution for ObjectNet, but a direct comparison with ImageNet would be highly beneficial.
- Sec 3.1 3) Agree top-5 might make classifiers' life easy, but it is more of an eval metric rather than training loss (the model still need to predict top-1 class correct during training). Meanwhile, it is not very clear why multi-label annotation would bias against model that "is spatially more precise but misses some objects". That model should be evaluated against detection benchmarks, rather than object recognition (image classification) datasets. The point about multi-label annotations potentially favoring models that generate correct labels without precise localization, as mentioned in the context of Beyer et al. (2020), further complicates the evaluation of pure recognition tasks.
- Sec 3.1 "bounding box annotation". For multiple objects nearby with the same category, the annotation would include all of them in one bounding box. This might leads to bad aspect ratio? (in general, bounding box would also vary more in aspect ratio than images, and feeding bounding box into a square CNN seems to be less ideal). This approach could introduce highly elongated or unusually shaped bounding boxes, which might present new challenges for CNNs typically trained on square or fixed aspect ratio inputs, potentially impacting their performance.
- Sec 3.1 "object recognition results". "AlexNet, VGG-19, and GoogLeNet have also been used", GoogLeNet should be "ResNet-152"? ObjectNet uses inception (GoogLeNet) v4 while authors use v3. The original ObjectNet paper (Barbu et al., 2019) indeed used Inception-v4, while the authors here used Inception-v3 (as stated in footnote 6), and the list of models mentioned in the text should accurately reflect those used by Barbu et al. for a precise comparison.
- Sec 3.2.1 "The higher the prediction accuracy, the higher the robustness against natural distortions". This is not necessarily true. Looking at Figure 3, it seems all models and both image / bounding box schemes would have decayed performance w.r.t distortion severity, and their slopes seem similar. While higher baseline accuracy can sometimes lead to better performance under mild distortions, true robustness is about the *rate* at which accuracy degrades as corruption severity increases. Figure 3 shows a clear drop in accuracy for all models and input types across severity levels, indicating that the models are indeed impacted by distortions, even if the bounding box inputs maintain a higher absolute accuracy.
- Sec 3.3 "Despite a large body of literature on whether and how much visual context benefits CNNs, the majority being focused on model accuracy, the matter has not been settled yet". "the matter" means context for robustness? please clarify. Clarifying that "the matter" refers specifically to the role of context in *robustness*, rather than just accuracy, would enhance the precision of the statement and align it better with the section's focus on robustness against perturbations.
- Sec 3.3 " Around 35.5% and 12.6% of the image pixels fall inside the object bounding box and the foreground object, respectively. Around 58.5% of the bounding box pixels fall inside the object mask.". here foreground object and object mask should be the same thing? if so, the numbers seem not matching (would expect box-to-image ratio * mask-to-box-ratio = mask-to-image ratio, or these numbers are normalized differently)? The provided percentages (32.7% image pixels in bounding box, 58.1% bounding box pixels in mask, as stated in the text) imply that the mask occupies approximately 0.327 * 0.581 = 0.190 (19%) of the total image area. The numbers stated in the original comment (35.5% and 12.6%) and the manuscript (32.7% and 58.1%) need careful reconciliation and clarification to ensure consistency.
- Figure 5: it is clear that seg-mask actually isn't robust to adversarial attacks (accuracy dropped significantly), which contradicts with the claim from authors. The left panel of Figure 5 shows that while segmentation masks lead to *relatively* higher accuracy compared to full image or bounding box inputs under adversarial attacks, the absolute accuracy still drops dramatically (e.g., from ~80% to below 20% for PGD-5). This significant drop suggests a lack of absolute robustness, even if the performance is comparatively better, which should be acknowledged in the interpretation of the results.
- Sec 3.3 "An attempt was made to tune the parameters to attain the best test accuracy in each case", could authors elaborate? Providing details on the tuning process, such as the specific hyperparameters optimized (e.g., learning rate, optimizer, epochs), the validation strategy used, and the range of values explored, would increase the reproducibility and transparency of the experiments.
- Sec 3.3.1. here the results seem to indicate segmask is more robust (less variations). Would the segmentation mask itself be indicative for object categories? It might be interesting to predict object classes directly from those masks as a baseline. The consistent improvement in robustness across geometric transformations when using segmentation masks, as shown in Figure 6, suggests that shape cues isolated by the mask are highly informative. Exploring a baseline where object classification relies solely on the geometric properties or raw pixel values of the segmentation mask could provide valuable insights into the contribution of shape information versus texture and context.

Final review:

The authors updated the manuscript and removed tuning experiment on ObjectNet, which addresses the major concern regarding the dataset license violation. I am still a bit concerned about the definition of "robustness" and the nuanced interpretation of performance under perturbations, as discussed in the minor comments. However, the paper overall does look good for ICLR publication, offering comprehensive analyses and valuable insights into the generalization and robustness of deep object recognition models on challenging real-world datasets like ObjectNet. The findings regarding the benefits of isolating objects and leveraging segmentation masks are particularly noteworthy.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
I like the main ideas articulated in the paper, but find the writing lacks some clarity: 

Summary of paper: The paper  takes as a starting point the study from Barbu et al where the robustness of object recognition pipelines to be able to handle distribution shifts are studied by testing ImageNet trained architectures against ObjectNet.  The main point in the current paper is that the performance degradation seen in Barbu et al is due to the fact that the CNNs were processing the image with entire image as context  and when one only provides a sub-window around the objects of interest the resulting performance improves significantly.  The paper also describes experiments with various synthetic distorted data and finally examines details of ObjectNet dataset to illustrate that there are images that are hard to categorize even for humans. Thus, the paper concludes that object recognition on ObjectNet is still hard to solve.

It is clear that by using bounding boxes or even removing background from those bounding boxes,  the performance will be better (since the training was on ImageNet with single objects). So, in a way, they are kind-of recreating the training distribution in order to improve the performance.

Main significance of the paper (Pros): Detailed study of performance of object recognition and the empirical finding that figure-ground segmentation may improve recognition.  Analysis of properties of ObjectNet and its challenges.

Originality/Novelty:  The paper is largely empirical and has a good discussion of the relevant background literature analyzing object recognition systems.  

Cons:  It has incremental insights. 

Clarity of paper: The description of the experiments are at times unclear.    The structure of the paper could be simplified with a table or diagram that illustrates the logic behind the experimentation and conclusion.  There are multiple datasets used, the training scheme is sometimes on ImageNet and tested on ObjectNet and sometimes on selected categories of ObjectNet and test on the rest of ObjectNet.

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:
I like the main ideas articulated in the paper, but find the writing lacks some clarity:

**Summary of paper:** The paper takes as a starting point the study from Barbu et al. (2019), where the robustness of deep object recognition pipelines to handle significant distribution shifts is thoroughly investigated by testing ImageNet-trained architectures against the ObjectNet dataset. The authors note the dramatic performance drop reported in the original ObjectNet paper. The main point in the current paper is that a substantial portion of this performance degradation seen in Barbu et al. is attributable to the fact that the convolutional neural networks (CNNs) were processing the entire image as context, rather than focusing on the primary object. When one isolates the object of interest by providing only a sub-window around it (i.e., using bounding boxes), the resulting performance improves significantly, recovering around 20-30% of the lost accuracy compared to using the full scene, as shown in Figure 2. This improvement is observed consistently across various models like AlexNet, VGG-19, and ResNet-152. The paper also describes extensive experiments with various synthetic distorted data, including common image corruptions like Gaussian noise, blur, and JPEG compression (Section 3.2.1), as well as adversarial attacks such as FGSM and PGD-5 (Section 3.2.2), demonstrating improved robustness when objects are isolated within bounding boxes or segmentation masks. Finally, the paper examines details of the ObjectNet dataset through a qualitative analysis, illustrating that there are a large number of images that are hard to categorize even for humans, as depicted in Figure 7 and Appendix D, often due to factors like unusual viewpoints, occlusions, or poor lighting. Thus, despite the observed gains, the paper concludes that object recognition on ObjectNet remains a challenging problem that is still far from solved for deep models. It is clear that by using bounding boxes or even removing background from those bounding boxes, the performance will be better, since the models were originally trained on ImageNet, which predominantly features single, isolated objects in less cluttered scenes (as discussed in Section 3.1 and Figure 1). In a way, the approach of cropping to bounding boxes or segmenting foreground objects is akin to recreating the training distribution conditions from ImageNet, thereby reducing the out-of-distribution challenge presented by ObjectNet's complex scenes.

**Main significance of the paper (Pros):** The paper provides a detailed and comprehensive empirical study of object recognition performance under various challenging conditions. A key empirical finding is that figure-ground segmentation, or at least limiting the input to the object's bounding box, may significantly improve both recognition accuracy and robustness. This is strongly supported by the experiments in Section 3.3, where models trained on segmented objects from MS COCO achieved 80.3% accuracy, compared to 66.9% for full images, and showed higher resilience to adversarial and noise corruptions (Figure 5). The consistent improvement across different models and corruption types, as shown in Figures 3 and 5, reinforces the importance of isolating the object. Furthermore, the paper offers a valuable analysis of the intrinsic properties and challenges of the ObjectNet dataset. The qualitative inspection in Section 3.4 highlights issues such as objects in motion, shadows, occlusions, atypical instances (e.g., the helmet in Fig. 34 row 5), objects requiring captions for recognition, and even instances of wrong labels (e.g., the pillow in Fig. 33 row 2). This deep dive into ObjectNet's characteristics provides crucial context for understanding why models struggle, even after isolating objects, and why human recognition can also be difficult.

**Originality/Novelty:** The paper is largely empirical, presenting a thorough re-evaluation of the ObjectNet benchmark and offering practical insights into how input preprocessing affects model performance and robustness. While the core idea of the benefits of object isolation is intuitive, the systematic quantification of its impact across various models (AlexNet, VGG-19, GoogLeNet, ResNet-152, Inception-v3, MNASNet), corruption types (14 natural image distortions), and attack methods (FGSM, PGD-5) adds significant empirical value. The paper also features a good and comprehensive discussion of the relevant background literature in Section 2, covering robustness against both synthetic and natural distribution shifts, the role of contextual interference, and insights from human vision, which enriches the overall contribution by contextualizing the findings within the broader field of computer vision.

**Cons:** Despite the extensive empirical analysis, the insights offered are arguably incremental. While the quantification of performance gains from object isolation is valuable, the notion that models trained on isolated objects perform better on isolated objects, especially when the original training data (ImageNet) largely consists of isolated objects, is not entirely novel. The paper acknowledges the existing body of work on distribution shifts and model generalization, and while it confirms these challenges on ObjectNet, it does not introduce fundamentally new theoretical concepts or architectural solutions for robust generalization. The significant performance gap, even after applying the proposed improvements, suggests that the underlying issues of generalization and robustness remain largely unaddressed at a deeper level.

**Clarity of paper:** The description of the experiments is at times unclear, which can make it challenging to follow the exact methodology and interpret some results. For instance, the precise details of data normalization and whether test-time data augmentation was consistently applied across all comparisons (e.g., between the authors' full image results and Barbu et al.'s results in Section 3.1) could be more explicitly stated. The statement "Our results using the full image are lower than Barbu et al.’s results using the full image (possibly because we do not utilize data augmentation)" (Section 3.1) underscores this ambiguity and leaves questions about comparability. The overall structure of the paper could also be simplified with a clearer table or diagram that illustrates the logic behind the diverse experimentation and conclusions. The paper moves between multiple datasets (ImageNet, ObjectNet, MNIST, FashionMNIST, MS COCO) and varying training schemes (e.g., ImageNet pre-trained models tested on ObjectNet; custom CNNs trained and tested on MNIST/FashionMNIST; ResNet-18 fine-tuned on selected COCO categories and tested on the rest of COCO). A guiding visual aid mapping these experimental setups to their specific contributions and showing how they connect to the main argument would greatly enhance readability and comprehension of the overarching narrative.

**Rating:** 6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
OVERVIEW:
The authors present a follow-up to the prior work of Barbu et al on the task of Object Recognition* (name confusion addressed in cons below). Barbu et al demonstrated that on a more realistic dataset like ObjectNet, models trained on a clean dataset like ImageNet suffer significant degradation. This work reduces the performance gap by cropping out the object using bounding box or mask information and running the recognition model on top of it. They do this for a variety of models (AlexNet, VGG-19, ResNet-152, Inception-v4, NASNet-A, PNASNet-5L) and transformations (image distortions, adversarial perturbations, context, geometric transformations). 

PROS:
- The paper is well-written and tackles an important topic of object recognition* in the wild. It tries to move away from the ImageNet driven approach that is currently present in the community to a more realistic scenario. 
- They build on the prior work of Barbu et al and are able to reduce performance gap demonstrated by Barbu et al by using bounding box or mask cropped images of the object of interest.
- They present a lot of experimental evaluation using a variety of models and transformations and demonstrate that their findings hold across all settings.

CONS:
- I agree with the authors that ImageNet with a single (or few) object present in the center of the image with clear foreground-background separation is unrealistic. ObjectNet is a better snapshot of the real world and models trained on ImageNet suffer in ObjectNet. However, the proposed approach to crop out the object using bounding box information or mask information is moving the data distribution from the real world setting of ObjectNet closer to the ideal setting of ImageNet. This then leads to an expected improvement in performance. I appreciate the thoroughness of the results and evaluation presented but it does not feel like a novel contribution in my opinion. 
- The authors present 4 future research directions in Section 4. I would be more willing to accept the paper if one of these research directions is incorporated as a contribution. For example, the last research direction of applying an object detection model trained with MS COCO on ObjectNet images instead of an image classification model trained with ImageNet is something that is doable. I would encourage the authors to even consider an object detection model trained on LVIS which has a larger number of object categories. This moves further away from object recognition* to object detection + classification but the latter is what we would typically encounter in a real-world scenario. Even here, I would need some contribution or novel analysis besides re-running current experiments with detection models.
- I strongly recommend a change of name to "Contemplating Real-World Object Classification" (no caps and classification instead of recognition). In my understanding, object recognition is a super-set of classification, detection, segmentation, etc. ImageNet leads to Image Classification models even if they are technically object classification. But sticking with the terminology of Object Recognition because it was used by Barbu et al is misleading. I would prefer Object Classification be used because that is the task of interest in this work.

REASON FOR RATING:
I think there is an interesting problem of real-world object classification that is of significant importance and this work moves a little closer to analyzing possible ways to reduce the performance gap from ImageNet to ObjectNet. However, their key contribution is somewhat expected (not novel) and needs some more work before being conference-paper ready.

UPDATE:
I have read the author feedback and the other reviews/discussions. I keep my original rating of 5. I think multiple authors raised the question of novelty relative to Barbu et al and the authors argue that they demonstrate the importance of context (whole image vs bounding box vs instance mask) for object recognition. Section 3.3 and Figure 5 is helpful in demonstrating it. However, the experimental setup is very limited (700 train + 300 test). COCO has 110K train and 5K val images and many more objects. If you argue that only 10 categories are common between COCO and ObjectNet, how many are common between LVIS and ObjectNet? I would strongly encourage the authors to leverage these pre-trained models and sharpen their message & contributions. I think they provide empirical justifications (important to the community) for expected results in moving from image to bounding box (same comment from multiple reviewers) but they need to de-emphasize that aspect and emphasize their results on context and robustness. A revision and resubmission to a different conference is encouraged.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Review:
OVERVIEW:
The authors present a comprehensive follow-up to the prior work of Barbu et al. (2019) on the task of Object Recognition* (a term which, as discussed below, could benefit from clarification). Barbu et al. initially demonstrated a significant performance degradation of deep models, primarily trained on clean datasets like ImageNet, when evaluated on a more realistic and challenging dataset like ObjectNet. This current work meticulously re-analyzes this performance gap, specifically demonstrating that it can be substantially reduced by pre-processing ObjectNet images to crop out the primary object of interest using either manually annotated bounding box or segmentation mask information, and then applying standard recognition models to these isolated object regions. The study systematically applies this approach across a variety of widely-used deep convolutional neural networks, including AlexNet, VGG-19, ResNet-152, Inception-v3, GoogLeNet, and MNASNet. Furthermore, the authors extend their analysis to various types of distribution shifts and transformations, examining robustness against common image distortions, adversarial perturbations, the influence of surrounding context, and geometric transformations.

PROS:
- The paper is well-written and tackles an important topic of object recognition* in the wild, directly addressing the generalization ability of deep models to real-world scenarios. It effectively highlights the limitations of an ImageNet-driven approach, which often focuses on isolated, centrally-located objects, and advocates for a more realistic evaluation using datasets like ObjectNet that feature objects in "daily life situations" and exhibit natural "distribution shifts" (as noted in the Abstract). The manuscript clearly articulates the need to move beyond potential "inherited biases" in prior studies relying solely on ImageNet variations.
- They build directly on the prior work of Barbu et al. and are able to reduce the dramatic performance gap demonstrated by Barbu et al. by using bounding box or mask cropped images of the object of interest. The authors demonstrate that applying models to isolated objects, rather than the entire scene, results in a substantial performance improvement of around 20-30% using their own code, recovering a notable portion of the original performance loss reported in Barbu et al. (Figure 2). This systematic re-evaluation, including the provision of new bounding box annotations for 18,574 images across 113 categories, is a valuable contribution that clarifies the precise nature of the performance drop on ObjectNet.
- They present a lot of experimental evaluation using a variety of models and transformations and demonstrate that their findings hold across all settings. The study's thoroughness is commendable, covering six different deep models and investigating robustness against 14 types of natural image distortions (e.g., Gaussian noise, various blurs, snow, JPEG compression), two adversarial attacks (FGSM and PGD-5), and geometric transformations (scaling, rotation, translation). For instance, Figure 3 and Table 1 clearly show that models are consistently more robust to common corruptions and adversarial attacks when applied to bounding box crops compared to full images. Similarly, Figure 6 illustrates improved robustness against geometric transformations when models are trained on segmented objects. This extensive empirical validation provides strong evidence for the benefits of object isolation in improving both accuracy and robustness.

CONS:
- I agree with the authors that ImageNet, with its typical setup of single (or few) objects present in the center of the image with clear foreground-background separation, is unrealistic for evaluating real-world generalization. ObjectNet certainly offers a better snapshot of the real world's visual complexity, and it is well-established that models trained predominantly on ImageNet suffer significant degradation on such out-of-distribution datasets. However, the proposed approach of cropping out the object using bounding box or mask information, while effective, fundamentally moves the input data distribution from the complex, real-world setting of ObjectNet closer to the idealized, single-object setting of ImageNet. This then leads to an expected improvement in performance, as the ImageNet-pretrained models are inherently designed and trained for this simpler, isolated object paradigm. The paper itself acknowledges this in Section 3.1, point 2, stating that the original performance drop "can be merely due to the fact that pretrained models on ImageNet have been trained on individual objects," and in Section 4, noting that "there is less variability in single objects compared to scenes containing multiple objects." While the quantification of this expected gain is valuable, the core finding regarding performance improvement through isolation does not strike as a novel contribution in its underlying principle.
- The authors present four future research directions in Section 4. While these are interesting avenues, I would be more willing to accept the paper if one of these research directions were incorporated as a concrete contribution within the current manuscript. For example, the last research direction of applying an object detection model (which includes classification) trained with a dataset like MS COCO or LVIS on ObjectNet images, instead of relying solely on image classification models trained on ImageNet, is something that is entirely feasible and directly addresses the "object detection" vs. "object recognition" confusion highlighted by the authors in Section 3.1. This approach would move further away from the traditional "object recognition*" task (as defined in this paper) towards a more holistic object detection + classification task, which is what we would typically encounter in a truly real-world scenario with cluttered scenes. Even here, I would require some additional contribution or novel analysis beyond simply re-running existing experiments with different model architectures; perhaps an analysis of how detection models fail or succeed on ObjectNet's specific challenges (e.g., object variations, occlusions, complex backgrounds).
- I strongly recommend a change of name to "Contemplating Real-World Object Classification" (using lowercase and "classification" instead of "recognition"). In my understanding, "object recognition" is a broader super-set encompassing various tasks like classification, detection, segmentation, and pose estimation. ImageNet is primarily used for Image Classification, even if the objects within are being "recognized." Sticking with the terminology of "Object Recognition" merely because it was used by Barbu et al. is misleading, especially since the paper's primary focus is on classifying isolated objects. The paper itself makes a clear distinction between "object detection" and "object recognition" (Section 3.1, point 1), and its methodology (classifying cropped objects) aligns much more closely with "object classification."

REASON FOR RATING:
I think there is an interesting and highly important problem of real-world object classification that the paper addresses, and this work moves a little closer to analyzing possible ways to reduce the performance gap from ImageNet to ObjectNet. The extensive empirical validation is a strength. However, their key contribution—that isolating objects improves classification performance on models pretrained on single-object datasets—is somewhat expected and therefore lacks sufficient novelty. It feels like a valuable empirical justification of a known principle, but needs some more foundational work or a stronger, more surprising finding before being conference-paper ready.

UPDATE:
I have read the author feedback and the other reviews/discussions. I keep my original rating of 5. I acknowledge the authors' argument that they demonstrate the importance of context (whole image vs. bounding box vs. instance mask) for object recognition, particularly in Section 3.3 and Figure 5. This is indeed a valuable line of inquiry. However, the experimental setup for these crucial context experiments, particularly the training of ResNet-18 models on MS COCO, is very limited (only 700 training images from 10 categories and 300 test images). This is a stark contrast to the scale of COCO itself, which boasts 110K train and 5K validation images, or LVIS which offers an even larger number of object categories and instances. If the argument is that only 10 categories are common between COCO and ObjectNet, further investigation into datasets like LVIS (which has a larger, more diverse set of categories) and their commonalities with ObjectNet would be beneficial and could provide a more robust demonstration. I would strongly encourage the authors to leverage larger, more representative pre-trained models and datasets for these context-related experiments to sharpen their message and contributions. While the paper provides empirical justifications (which are important to the community) for expected results in moving from image to bounding box (a comment echoed by multiple reviewers), they need to de-emphasize this aspect and instead emphasize their results on context and robustness with more substantial experimental backing. A revision and resubmission to a different conference would allow for this necessary expansion and strengthening of the core contributions.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

title: Provably robust classification of adversarial examples with detection

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
Summary: 
This paper deals with the problem of bounding the amount of errors that a model can make when attacked by an adversary limited to small perturbation of an input image. Similarly to previous paper, it proposes to extend the classifier with a detector to identify adversarial examples. As opposed to previous papers, the authors suggest to perform formal verification to prove that the samples are either robustly classified or abstained on (as opposed to testing the classifier + detector adversarially which would have left open the question of whether or not the attack against the pair was done in an adequate way). The method used for this is based on the simple IBP algorithm but the authors propose a proper encoding of the max(abstain_logit, gt_logit). They also follow the IBP strategy of using the bound resulting from verification as part of the training objective in order to encourage the network to behave properly.
Experimental results are reported on MNIST and CIFAR-10, and shows that there are scenarios in which the behaviour caused by this algorithm might be desirable.The experimental protocol for training network with the proposed method is described in significant amount of details and it should be possible to reproduce experimental results based on the description.

The crux of the paper lies in redefining the way accuracy should be computed in the case of an abstain option and adjusting verification and robust training methodologies for it. The accuracy is computed in the following way:
* For natural images, the prediction is considered correct only if the output is the correct class.
* For perturbed image, the prediction can be either the correct class OR the abstain class.
This might be sensible, but unless I'm mistaken, this could lead to degenerate cases where the network could achieve a verified error of 0 while having a standard error of 100% (see below). It's regrettable that we lose the property of the verified error rate being an upper bound on the standard error rate.

If I'm not mistaken, the results in Table 1 are hard to evaluate because the methods proposed by the authors is the only one that considers an abstain class, which gives it leeway to "cheat". A pathological example of this would be a network always outputting "abstain". If I understand correctly, it would be classified as having 0% verified error, while having 100% standard error. A moderate version of this is what can be observed for all the pairs (except CIFAR10-eps=16) : standard error goes up compared to IBP while verified error goes down. 
Figure 1. is however quite helpful in showing that there might be some benefit in the proposed approach if the goal of an application leans more towards "Never making bad predicitions" rather than "Always making good predicitions".

Comments:
- I am not entirely sure of the premise of the papers that "adversarial examples is something that we can defend against". I am not convinced that people are worried about an attacker crafting attacks to fool their classifier. The problem is that we want our classifiers to be robust to small perturbation, because that's a desirable property and adversarial examples is a sign that this property is not met. 

There might be some potential extension to this paper in the direction of OOD detection. Out of Distribution examples tend to make the classifier perform poorly. There is also a glut of methods that claims to be able to detect out of distribution examples without any formal guarantees so I would encourage the authors to look in that direction for extensions to their work.

Opinion: The paper is quite interesting to read, and as far as I can tell is the first one to apply verification methods to the detection of adversarial examples. This is a welcome contribution as opposed to all the methods that claim to detect attacks but can not provide any guarantees.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
This paper deals with the problem of bounding the amount of errors that a model can make when attacked by an adversary limited to small perturbation of an input image, specifically within an (cid:96)∞-norm bounded ball. Similarly to previous papers, it proposes to extend the classifier with a detector to identify adversarial examples. The introduction effectively highlights the practical challenge that most detection methods have been broken by adaptive attacks, necessitating verifiable performance.

As opposed to previous papers, the authors suggest to perform formal verification to prove that the samples are either robustly classified or abstained on (as opposed to testing the classifier + detector adversarially which would have left open the question of whether or not the attack against the pair was done in an adequate way). The manuscript explicitly states that, to the best of their knowledge, this is the first method to extend certification techniques by considering detection while providing provable verification. The method used for this is based on the simple Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) algorithm, but the authors propose a proper encoding of the `max(abstain_logit, gt_logit)` in their verification scheme (Eq. 9), which is then lower-bounded by the convex optimization presented in Theorem 2. This formulation, analytically solvable via Algorithm 1, is a key technical contribution allowing for the provable guarantees. They also follow the IBP strategy of using the bound resulting from verification as part of the training objective (Eq. 18) in order to encourage the network to behave properly.

Experimental results are reported on MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets for various perturbation sizes (e.g., (cid:15) = 8/255 and 16/255 on CIFAR-10), and shows that there are scenarios in which the behaviour caused by this algorithm might be desirable. The experimental protocol for training networks with the proposed method is described in a significant amount of detail in Appendix B, including the use of Adam optimizer, learning rate schedules, and various ramp-down/ramp-up procedures for parameters like κ, (cid:15), and η/¯η (Section 5.1). This level of detail should make it possible to reproduce experimental results based on the description, which is a strong point for this work.

The crux of the paper lies in redefining the way accuracy should be computed in the case of an abstain option and adjusting verification and robust training methodologies for it. As detailed in Section 3.1, the accuracy is computed in the following way:
*   For natural images, the prediction is considered correct only if the output is the correct class.
*   For perturbed image, the prediction can be either the correct class OR the abstain class.
This might be sensible, as the authors argue that for adversarial inputs, both correct classification and abstention prevent misclassification into a wrong class. However, it is regrettable that we lose the property of the verified error rate being an upper bound on the standard error rate, as this could lead to degenerate cases where the network could achieve a verified error of 0 while having a standard error of 100% by simply abstaining on all inputs. While the authors attempt to mitigate this by including the `Lnatural(x, y; θ)` term in the overall loss (Eq. 14) and stating that it "prevents the degenerate solution of always classifying all images in the abstain class" (Section 5.1), the fundamental conceptual shift in what "verified error" signifies for natural vs. adversarial inputs remains.

If I'm not mistaken, the results in Table 1 are hard to evaluate because the methods proposed by the authors is the only one that considers an abstain class, which gives it leeway to "cheat". A pathological example of this would be a network always outputting "abstain". If I understand correctly, it would be classified as having 0% verified error, while having 100% standard error. A moderate version of this is what can be observed for all the pairs (except CIFAR10-eps=16): standard error goes up compared to IBP while verified error goes down. For instance, on CIFAR-10 with (cid:15) = 8/255, the IBP with detection method reports a standard error of 55.60% (compared to IBP's 53.69%) but a verified error of 63.63% (compared to IBP's 69.92%). This implies that some natural misclassifications are being converted into abstentions, which is explicitly discussed by the authors in Section 5.2, point (1): they note "30.5% abstain and 25.6% ‘wrong-class misclassiﬁcation’ (other than abstain and the correct class) in IBP-with-detection, with that of 53.7% ‘wrong-class’ misclassiﬁcation in IBP on natural CIFAR-10 images". The footnote `†` in Table 1 also acknowledges this difficulty, stating that direct comparison is difficult due to the detection capability.

Figure 1. is however quite helpful in showing that there might be some benefit in the proposed approach if the goal of an application leans more towards "Never making bad predictions" rather than "Always making good predictions". The decomposition of accuracy and verified accuracy on CIFAR-10 clearly illustrates how the abstain class contributes to the overall performance metrics, especially in detecting "difficult" natural images and increasing the robustness certificate for adversarial inputs. This visual aid clarifies the trade-offs involved and the utility of the abstain option.

Comments:
- I am not entirely sure of the premise of the papers that "adversarial examples is something that we can defend against". I am not convinced that people are worried about an attacker crafting attacks to fool their classifier. The problem is that we want our classifiers to be robust to small perturbation, because that's a desirable property and adversarial examples is a sign that this property is not met. While the paper's opening in the abstract notes that detection methods have often failed against adaptive attacks, the underlying concern about general robustness to small perturbations remains a fundamental issue that may not be fully addressed by detection mechanisms alone.

There might be some potential extension to this paper in the direction of OOD detection. Out of Distribution examples tend to make the classifier perform poorly. There is also a glut of methods that claims to be able to detect out of distribution examples without any formal guarantees so I would encourage the authors to look in that direction for extensions to their work. The authors do briefly mention existing work on OOD detection (Bitterwolf et al., 2020) in their introduction, suggesting an awareness of this related field. Applying a similar provable verification framework to OOD detection could be a highly impactful future direction, addressing a similar gap in formal guarantees found in that domain.

Opinion: The paper is quite interesting to read, and as far as I can tell is the first one to apply verification methods to the detection of adversarial examples, providing provable guarantees for this joint classification/detection task. This is a welcome contribution as opposed to all the methods that claim to detect attacks but can not provide any guarantees, especially given the history of detection methods being circumvented by adaptive attacks. The technical formulation for extending IBP, particularly Theorem 2 and Algorithm 1 for analytical solution, is robust. Furthermore, the detailed "stabilizing the training procedure" in Section 5.1 demonstrates a thorough approach to making the proposed provable method practically effective.

7: Good paper, accept

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes a new defense method based on adding a reject class to a classifier trained for robust classification. The paper has merits, it is formally sound and it improves the SOA. However, my rating is not so high for the following reasons:

- Only a short paragraph at the end is devoted to the natural-vs-robust error trade-off and the comparison is done only with IBP (another defense method of 2018). In my opinion this important trade-off is being neglected. What is the point of developing classifiers that are robust to AEs when they are not robust to other normal images (like those found in the test set)?
- Adding a reject class may not seem fair. Particularly, the paper states (p.4) "the classification/detection tasks is considered successful if the input is classified either as the correct class y or as the abstain class a; as both cases prevent misclassification of the adversarially perturbed input as a wrong class". I do not agree with that; it should be considered successful only if the input is classified as the correct class y, period. The "abstain" class is just a reject option, which is not the same as the correct classification. One can always resort to rejection to improve the error rate in the non-rejected part of the dataset. When there is a reject option in the system the figure of merit should be the ratio between error and rejection rate, while in your case you are using only error as the figure of merit. What would be the error-reject trade-off curve of the base classifier in these same datasets? 

Also, I could not understand the relative weights lambda 1 and 2 in Eq. 14. Are they correct? shouldn't there be a weight also for the first term Lrobust? Likewise in Eq. 18 you state that for lambda1=0 the training reduces to that of Gowal but the weight terms are different in Eqs. 18 and 7.

Minor: 
- When referencing an equation you should capitalise the first letter E. 
- "plotted in 2"
- "network can increase robust"

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
This paper proposes a novel defense method that integrates a reject class into a classifier designed for provably robust classification. The core idea of augmenting existing certified defense mechanisms with an "abstain/detection" option, as outlined in the abstract and Section 3, is innovative and merits attention. The formal development, particularly the extension of Interval Bound Propagation (IBP) for this new robust objective (Theorem 1, Theorem 2, and Algorithm 1 for the verification problem), appears to be sound. The authors demonstrate that this method improves the state-of-the-art (SOA) in certified robustness, especially for larger perturbation sizes on datasets like CIFAR-10. For instance, Table 1 shows that for CIFAR-10 with $\epsilon = 8/255$, the proposed IBP with detection achieves a verified error of 63.63%, which is a notable improvement over IBP in isolation (69.92%) and even competitive with or better than some other reported methods from the literature such as Balunovic & Vechev (2020) at 71.33%. This suggests a valuable contribution to the field of adversarial robustness.

However, my rating is not so high for the following reasons. Only a short paragraph at the end (Section 5.3) is devoted to the natural-vs-robust error trade-off, and the comparison is done primarily only with IBP (another defense method of 2018). While Figure 2 and Figure 3 in the appendix do illustrate these trade-off curves for MNIST and CIFAR-10 across various perturbation sizes, the discussion and analysis of these crucial results feel underdeveloped. In my opinion, this important trade-off is being neglected. The practical utility of a robust classifier is heavily dependent on its performance on clean, natural images. What is the point of developing classifiers that are robust to adversarial examples when they are not robust to other normal images (like those found in the test set)? A more thorough analysis of how the proposed method balances these two objectives, perhaps with comparisons to a broader range of recent provably robust methods beyond just vanilla IBP or IBP-CROWN, would significantly strengthen the empirical evaluation.

Adding a reject class, while a valid strategy for safety-critical applications, may not seem entirely fair when comparing "robust error" directly against methods without a reject option. Particularly, the paper states on page 4, "the classification/detection tasks is considered successful if the input is classified either as the correct class y or as the abstain class a; as both cases prevent misclassification of the adversarially perturbed input as a wrong class." I do not agree with that; it should be considered successful only if the input is classified as the correct class y, period. The "abstain" class is just a reject option, which is not the same as the correct classification. Conflating abstention with correct classification, especially in the context of "veriﬁed error" (e.g., in Table 1, where the IBP w/ detection "veriﬁed err" is presented as a direct improvement over IBP alone), can be misleading. One can always resort to rejection to improve the error rate in the non-rejected part of the dataset. When there is a reject option in the system, the figure of merit should be carefully defined, perhaps as the accuracy on non-rejected samples, or the rejection rate at a target accuracy. While Figure 1 provides a decomposition of accuracy into 'correct class', 'abstain', and 'misclassification' for both natural and adversarial images, it doesn't fully address the trade-off inherent in a system with a reject option from an evaluation standpoint. What would be the error-reject trade-off curve of the proposed classifier (IBP with detection) in these same datasets, clearly showing how the rate of abstention impacts the true classification accuracy? This would provide a more transparent and comparable measure of performance.

Also, I could not understand the relative weights $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ in Eq. 14. Are they correct? The equation is presented as $L = L_{robust} + \lambda_1 L_{abstain}^{robust} + \lambda_2 L_{natural}$, implying an implicit weight of 1 for $L_{robust}$. This structure becomes less clear when compared to Eq. 19, which introduces a ramp-down parameter $\kappa$ that modulates both the robust and natural loss terms differently: $L = (1 - \kappa)(\overline{L}_{robust}(x, y; \theta) + \lambda_1 \overline{L}_{abstain}^{robust}(x, y; \theta)) + \kappa \lambda_2 L_{natural}(x, y; \theta)$. This reintroduces a global scaling for the robust terms. Furthermore, the statement in Section 4 that "setting $\lambda_1 = 0$ and $\gamma = \lambda_2$ - and incorporation of a ramp-down process by parameter $\kappa$ as detailed in Section 5 - reduces the training in Eq. 18 to that of Gowal et al. (2018) without detection" is problematic. In Gowal et al. (2018), Eq. 7 defines their objective as minimize $\theta \sum_{(x,y) \in D} (1 - \kappa)\mathcal{L}_{xent}(-\mathbf{J}^{\text{IBP}}_{\epsilon,\theta}(x,y), y) + \kappa\gamma\mathcal{L}_{xent}(f_{\theta}(x), y)$. The weight terms are clearly different in Eq. 18/19 of this paper versus Eq. 7 of Gowal et al. (2018). Specifically, the role of $\gamma$ from Gowal et al. and $\lambda_2$ in this paper, especially when $\lambda_1=0$, needs to be explicitly aligned and explained for a fair comparison and understanding of the loss function's behavior. The current explanation leads to confusion regarding the exact equivalence.

Minor:
When referencing an equation, you should capitalise the first letter (e.g., "Eq. 14" instead of "eq. 14"). Similarly, "plotted in 2" should be "plotted in Figure 2," and "network can increase robust" should be "network can increase robustness."

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Review:
This paper proposes a new defense method based on adding a reject class to a classifier trained for robust classification. The paper has merits, it is formally sound and it improves the SOA. However, my rating is not so high for the following reasons:

- Only a short paragraph at the end is devoted to the natural-vs-robust error trade-off and the comparison is done only with IBP (another defense method of 2018). In my opinion this important trade-off is being neglected. What is the point of developing classifiers that are robust to AEs when they are not robust to other normal images (like those found in the test set)?
- Adding a reject class may not seem fair. Particularly, the paper states (p.4) "the classification/detection tasks is considered successful if the input is classified either as the correct class y or as the abstain class a; as both cases prevent misclassification of the adversarially perturbed input as a wrong class". I do not agree with that; it should be considered successful only if the input is classified as the correct class y, period. The "abstain" class is just a reject option, which is not the same as the correct classification. One can always resort to rejection to improve the error rate in the non-rejected part of the dataset. When there is a reject option in the system the figure of merit should be the ratio between error and rejection rate, while in your case you are using only error as the figure of merit. What would be the error-reject trade-off curve of the base classifier in these same datasets? 

Also, I could not understand the relative weights lambda 1 and 2 in Eq. 14. Are they correct? shouldn't there be a weight also for the first term Lrobust? Likewise in Eq. 18 you state that for lambda1=0 the training reduces to that of Gowal but the weight terms are different in Eqs. 18 and 7.

Minor: 
- When referencing an equation you should capitalise the first letter E. 
- "plotted in 2"
- "network can increase robust"

Rating:
6: Marginally above acceptance threshold

Review:
##########################################################################

Summary: 

In this paper, the authors propose an additional "abstain/detection" loss term into training, so that the classifier can either robustly classify or detect an adversarial attack. They extend the interval bound propagation method for certified robustness under L_infity perturbations (a simple bounding in changes of the weight after each NN layer application, using a box constraint). Hyperparameters in the objective trade-off between clean and adversarial accuracy. 

##########################################################################

Reasons for score: 
 
Overall, I think the paper is borderline. I like the idea of adding an "abstain" error term in the loss during training, and I like that the authors have derived some (simple) bounds on the min-max formulations. They show some benefit of addition of abstain/detection in the training. However, my major concern is that if these results could also be obtained without adding these losses in the objective, but by simply comparing "closeness" or confusion in the class labels from the classifier, and abstaining from classification as a post-processing step instead. What is the benefit of adding this to the training? 
 
##########################################################################

Pros: 

The authors are trying to break the loop of adversarial training and subsequent adaptive attacks, by developing "provably robust" methods.

The idea of incorporating an "abstain" loss term at the training stage, and trading it off with the true error in classification is interesting. They further modify the loss slightly to exclude true and abstain classes (perhaps, driven by experiments). 

They obtain (simple) convex upper bounds on the loss functions and minimize these. 

##########################################################################

Cons: 

1. Choice of the loss functions: why the specific choice? Loss for misusing abstain class or mislabeling the true label could be something more general. 

2. Choice of hyperparameters: How does one select \lamba_1 and \lambda_2 in practice? The authors mention a few practical ways (also used by authors in related works), e.g., ramp down of \kappa etc. Why are these good choices?

3. Clarity in the writing: The paper is unclear at many places- for example, it is nowhere mentioned that the maximum weighted class is finally chosen, and the interval bounds are not propagated at this last layer. Algorithm 1 is explained nowhere in the paper, except in a psuedocode. 

4. Can similar benefits in adversarial robustness be obtained by post-processing an adversarially trained model?
 
##########################################################################

Questions during rebuttal period: 

 
Please address and clarify the cons above
