reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
*** Summary ***

The paper investigates emergent gesture-based communication in Embodied Multi-Agent Populations. A noticeable feature of the paper is that it investigates emergent communication in the case of non-uniform distribution of intents and costly communication (i.e. agents are penalized for effort). The authors find that in certain scenarios, these conditions may lead to communication generalization of learned communication strategies to new, previously unseen agents.

*** Relevance, Originality, Novelty *** 

I believe that the paper meets ICLR conference standards when it comes to the importance of the problem considered, the originality of the approach and the novelty of the proposed ideas.

*** Clarity ***

The paper is, in general, clearly written and is a pleasure to read. Figure 4 is deeply confusing, however. As mentioned in the appendix, the actual number of timesteps is equal to 5 and is upsampled during visualization. I believe that it is important to mention it right away (i.e. in the image description).
Otherwise, the reader can easily misunderstand the setup after seeing this picture.

*** Quality ***

The quality of the experimental support is, unfortunately, the weakest part of the contribution.

The authors introduce a number of exciting ideas, all of which make the communication learning setting more realistic. Specifically, the authors are studying communication in an embodied setting, Zipfian intent distribution, and the idea of effort-based action cost.

At the same time, while the ideas sound very promising in theory, in practice, the complications introduced by the embodied setting complicate the interpretation of the obtained results when it comes to the effects of Zipfian intent distribution and effort-based action costs.

In order to mitigate the difficulties, authors propose additional measures: providing explicit feature information to the outside observer (i.e. the observer directly gets the action effort); using "torque pretraining" to make the model more familiar with the reward landscape, dramatically reducing the number of actions.
These measures, in my opinion, are significantly detrimental to the potential impact of the paper. Providing effort information directly they makes the setting less realistic, defeating the purpose of the embodied approach.  Torque curriculum makes the results very specific to the considered setting.

There are other significant limitations of the experimental results that are probably indirectly induced by the embodied setting. Specifically, figure 2 and table 1 report no variability measures for the obtained result, and it's not clear, how many runs were made to obtain these results. Moreover, the agent population considered in these experiments is 10 (mentioned in the appendix). With such a small population, it is not surprising that the observer can simply memorize the individual patterns, and is not pressured to infer the underlying structure.  It seems very natural, therefore, to increase the number of agents in the simulation.
I suspect that these limitations are a consequence of the fact that training in the multidimentional setting is very costly and time-consuming.

Moreover, some of the limitations I describe in the "technical soundness" section could have been controlled for in a simpler setting.

Overall, all things considered, it's not clear what are the insights gained from the embodied approach.

*** Technical soundness ***

There are certain technical problems with the contribution:

Firstly, the justification for why Zipfian intents, together with effort-based penalty should lead to generalization is not rigorous (paragraph 1, section 4.1), and not completely convincing.

One of the sentences in the intuitive justification raises particularly serious concerns. The authors say "Coupled, [Zipfian intents and Energy regularization] incentivize an inverse relationship between energy exertion and intent frequency, assigning minimum energy trajectories to maximally occurring intents.". This assumes that energy levels are "occupied" by a limited number of actions. Why that would be true is entirely unclear to me.

For example, for any action with a certain energy level, there is a mirror action (with all joint torques multiplied by -1) with exactly the same energy consumption. Even if for some reason such specific pair of distinct actions with exactly the same effort 
is impossible in a given setting, given how high the degree of freedom of the system is, it is still easy to imagine that every energy level offers a plethora of actions to choose from.

This raises a question of why then we see good performance in 2-action scenario. There is an alternative explanation that was not explored: there is a unique action with zero effort: doing nothing.
I suspect that it may be the only special case, where energy level is mapped uniquely onto an action (i.e. there is no different action with the same (0) energy penalty).

Another alternative explanation that must be addressed is the exposure bias. Rare intents are sampled less frequently, therefore the model has less time to optimize its actions for that intent. This may explain some of the differences in energy costs between the rare and frequent actions.

Lastly, the architecture choice and problem setting formalization also raise certain concerns. Specifically, the sender only receives joint positions and angles at time t, with no history. This does not seem to be an appropriate state specification. Coupled with the fact that the network is a simple feedforward architecture (no recurrence), this 
limits the number of possible jestures that can be generated.

*** Conclusion ***

The paper addresses a highly relevant problem and proposes a number of interesting ideas. Unfortunately, in my opinion, 
some of the proposed ideas (studying communication in an embodied setting, Zipfian intent distribution, costly actions), when introduced together, are not interacting well and are hindering result interpretation.

As authors mention, using an embodied setting with multidimentional continuous action and observation spaces makes the problem extremely challenging. Therefore, when we see only partial success or no success in learning, it becomes difficult to identify the true source of the problem. That is, it is unclear if we are hitting a fundamental limit of what can be achieved by the combination of the Zipfian intent + costly actions, or is whether it a just a limitation of the architectures/algorithms that the authors used, when those algorithms are applied in a challenging multidimentional setting.

The latter concern is exacerbated by some technical questions. In particular, the choice of a feedforward architecture seems extremely limiting, when combined with the state description that the authors used. The state space for the policy (sender) agent only includes the current joint configuration, without the history. This renders a large number of actions impossible (i.e. the "pendulum-like" wave of a hand becomes impossible, since when the hand returns to the middle after the first half-swing, the policy would act as if the action just started).

Lastly, the logic behind the main hypothesis is not fully theoretically supported, and the alternative explanations are not addressed.

Overall, I believe that the contribution is extremely promising, but it still requires some polishing and potential restructuring in order to be up to the standards of the ICLR conference. I do deeply hope to see this paper improved and published in the future, as I believe that after some improvements, it will be of interest to a large portion of the community.

*** Suggestions ***

I believe that the paper can be made much stronger if the authors take a step back and start with a simplified communication game setting. E.g. instead of actually producing an action trajectory, agents could pick among a number of actions with pre-defined effort levels, or they can separately pick the gesture and the amount of effort to allocate to that gesture (i.e. a subtle hand-wave vs flailing one's arms in the air).
This would allow to explore the benefits and limitations of the proposed Zipfian intent+Costly actions approach. In that case, demonstrating that the approach also works in an emboddied setting would be a beautiful cherry on top. Alternatively, it is possible to "boost" the embodiment part of the paper, 
e.g. by pre-training the agents on some other tasks, thus making certain actions more natural, etc. Currently, it just seems that embodiment itself is not adding additional insights, but complicating the experiments.

Adding noize (mentioned in the appendix) is a very good idea as intuitively, it seems that it is the only reason why the agents don't converge on an array of extremely subtle actions with near-zero effort. I believe that it was not discussed enough in the paper and maybe its importance is somewhat overlooked.

*** Questions *** 

- "Furthermore, with an increase in task complexity to 10 concepts, the external observer never performs better than this baseline strategy" I thought the baseline accuracy was 0.34, so it seems that with curriculum the external observer does outperform the baseline.
- Apart from the zero-effort action, why can't the agents converge on minimally distinguishable actions with the same effort level? 

*** Typos and other minor suggestions ***

"execution decentralized" -> "decentralized execution"
"Agent reward" verbally described as a "function of state and actions taken", but the formula is written as if that it is a function of observation and actions taken.

Table 1 is slightly confusing to read, it may be better to make it broader so that the phrase "Train Input" does not break into two lines.

Conflicting notation: N is used as the number of agents on page 3, but later N refers to the number of concepts. 10 agents - not enough to infer the latent variable, especially if no randomness is added to the agents.

I personally believe that the use of the term "zero-shot" in this setting is not ideal. The observer is trained on a sample of communication protocols and is then tested on a held-out set. 
In my view, it's analogous to "generalization performance", not "zero-shot" performance. I understand that the authors are referring to another study that did use "zero-shot", so this consideration did not affect my score.
Before the name for this problem setting is thoroughly established, it may be possible to use "out of sample coordination" or some other option.

*** Update after rebuttal ***

I have read the rebuttal and I deeply appreciate the detailed response by the authors. I especially appreciate the introduction of a number of experiments on a simple domain that help to illustrate the main point of the paper.

At the same time, I believe that some serious issues still remain unresolved. For example, my main concern remains: I believe that wrapping the problem in the embodied setting is not introducing additional insights. I must clarify that I fully agree that studying how embodiment affects cognition/behavior is an extremely important and exciting area of research. But in the present paper, the models have no chance to benefit from embodiment (since there is no prior / shared embodied experience), but rather have to solve the task despite being placed in an embodied setting.

The main insight (in my opinion) is the observation that Zipfian intent distribution together with energy costs could be a good basis for zero-shot communication. I think that additional experiments that the authors introduced help to strengthen this point, although more experiments could still be beneficial (e.g. systematically varying the population size), as well as a more thorough theoretical discussion. At the same time, the limitations of the main "embodied" experiment remain (most importantly, the fact that fairly high accuracy can be achieved because of the unique "do nothing" action trajectory). 

In short, a large part of the paper (on embodiment) contributes relatively little in terms of its impact and conclusions that can be drawn from it, which necessarily limits the extent to which the main insightful point can be explored. The main point is truly interesting, however, which makes the paper borderline.

Overall, I believe that the paper is extremely promising and I would love to see an expanded version published. I feel very torn about the decision, but at the moment, I believe that the paper is still below the acceptance threshold, although only marginally. I am happy to adjust the score up, and I regret that I can not switch it to an "accept" recommendation.

As a minor aside - the newly introduced Colab Notebook does not fully run and crashes at the cell #4 (model loading), so I can not fully explore the newly introduced experiments. That being said, I think that after fixing, this resource can be very useful in the future. This minor issue did not affect my evaluation.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
## Expanded Review

*** Summary ***

The paper investigates emergent gesture-based communication in Embodied Multi-Agent Populations, specifically focusing on agents that learn to communicate by actuating their joints in a 3D environment. A noticeable feature of the paper is that it investigates emergent communication in the case of non-uniform distribution of intents and costly communication (i.e. agents are penalized for effort). The authors find that in certain scenarios, these conditions may lead to communication generalization of learned communication strategies to new, previously unseen agents.

*** Relevance, Originality, Novelty *** 

I believe that the paper meets ICLR conference standards when it comes to the importance of the problem considered, the originality of the approach, and the novelty of the proposed ideas. The authors explicitly state that "To the best of our knowledge however, we are first to explore deep reinforcement learning for emergent communication in the context of embodied agents using articulated motion" (Page 1), which highlights the innovative nature of their setup. The exploration of non-verbal, embodied communication in a deep RL framework is a significant and timely direction for the field.

*** Clarity ***

The paper is, in general, clearly written and is a pleasure to read. The introduction effectively sets the stage by highlighting the limitations of discrete cheap-talk channels and motivating the shift to embodied, continuous communication. However, Figure 4 is deeply confusing. As mentioned in the appendix, the actual number of timesteps for policy learning is equal to 5, and the trajectories are upsampled during visualization (Appendix A.3). I believe that it is important to mention this right away in the image description of Figure 4, and similarly for Figure 5. Otherwise, a reader can easily misunderstand the setup and assume the agents are generating much longer, more complex trajectories than they actually are, which impacts the interpretation of the results, especially concerning the distinctness of gestures.

*** Quality ***

The quality of the experimental support is, unfortunately, the weakest part of the contribution.

The authors introduce a number of exciting ideas, all of which make the communication learning setting more realistic. Specifically, the authors are studying communication in an embodied setting, Zipfian intent distribution, and the idea of effort-based action cost. The theoretical grounding for these choices, particularly the combination of energy cost and non-uniform intent distribution to enable Zero-Shot (ZS) communication, is appealing (Section 1).

At the same time, while the ideas sound very promising in theory, in practice, the complications introduced by the embodied setting — which involves a high-dimensional continuous action space for joint actuation (Section 3) — complicate the interpretation of the obtained results when it comes to the effects of Zipfian intent distribution and effort-based action costs. The inherent difficulty of optimizing in such a complex, non-convex landscape makes it challenging to disentangle whether observed outcomes are due to the core theoretical principles or simply optimization hurdles.

In order to mitigate the difficulties, authors propose additional measures: providing explicit feature information to the outside observer (i.e. the observer directly gets the action effort, as described in Section 4.2); using "torque pretraining" (a curriculum where actors first minimize torque uniformly, Section 5.3) to make the model more familiar with the reward landscape, dramatically reducing the number of actions. These measures, in my opinion, are significantly detrimental to the potential impact of the paper. Providing effort information directly to the observer makes the setting less realistic and essentially engineers the communication channel by giving explicit access to the latent structure. This defeats the original purpose of studying *emergent* communication in an embodied approach, where the agents should ideally discover and exploit this structure autonomously. Furthermore, the torque curriculum makes the results very specific to the considered setting, potentially limiting the generalizability of the proposed training improvements.

There are other significant limitations of the experimental results that are probably indirectly induced by the embodied setting and its associated computational costs. Specifically, Figure 2 and Table 1 report no variability measures for the obtained result, and it's not clear how many independent runs were made to obtain these results. In deep reinforcement learning, especially with continuous action spaces and complex optimization landscapes, reporting mean and standard deviation over multiple random seeds is crucial for assessing the robustness and statistical significance of findings. Moreover, the agent population considered in these experiments is 10 (mentioned in Appendix A.3). With such a small population, and considering that 80% of these agents are used for observer training (Appendix A.3), it is not surprising that the observer can simply memorize the individual patterns. This setup does not sufficiently pressure the observer to infer the underlying *generalizable* structure, thus weakening the claim of true Zero-Shot coordination. It seems very natural, therefore, to increase the number of agents in the simulation to properly test generalization to truly novel partners. I suspect that these limitations are a consequence of the fact that training in the multidimensional setting is very costly and time-consuming.

Moreover, some of the limitations I describe in the "technical soundness" section could have been controlled for more effectively in a simpler setting, allowing for clearer causal attribution. Overall, all things considered, it's not clear what are the novel insights gained specifically *from the embodied approach* itself, beyond the general concept of energy-cost and Zipfian distribution. The complexities introduced by embodiment seem to obscure rather than illuminate the core mechanisms.

*** Technical soundness ***

There are certain technical problems with the contribution:

Firstly, the justification for why Zipfian intents, together with effort-based penalty should lead to generalization is not rigorous (paragraph 1, section 4.1), and not completely convincing. The core claim, "Coupled, they incentivize an inverse relationship between energy exertion and intent frequency, assigning minimum energy trajectories to maximally occurring intents," assumes a unique or at least clearly separable mapping between intent ranks and energy levels.

One of the sentences in the intuitive justification raises particularly serious concerns. The authors say "Coupled, [Zipfian intents and Energy regularization] incentivize an inverse relationship between energy exertion and intent frequency, assigning minimum energy trajectories to maximally occurring intents.". This assumes that energy levels are "occupied" by a limited number of actions. Why that would be true is entirely unclear to me in a continuous, high-dimensional action space. The paper itself acknowledges "action degeneracies" in the discrete domain (Appendix A.5), where multiple actions map to the same energy cost. This problem is exacerbated in a continuous domain.

For example, for any action with a certain energy level, there is a mirror action (with all joint torques multiplied by -1) with exactly the same energy consumption, according to the L2 torque loss (Equation 2). Even if for some reason such specific pair of distinct actions with exactly the same effort is impossible in a given setting, given how high the degree of freedom of the system is (e.g., a robot arm with 4 articulated joints, each with 3 DOFs, as described in Appendix A.1), it is still easy to imagine that every energy level offers a plethora of distinct actions to choose from. This degeneracy makes it difficult for independently trained agents to converge on *the same* mapping from intents to specific energy-level-actions, even if they agree on the *ordering* of energy levels.

This raises a question of why then we see good performance in the 2-intent scenario (Table 1, Figure 4). There is an alternative explanation that was not explored: there is a unique action with zero effort: doing nothing (i.e., minimal joint velocities). I suspect that it may be the only special case, where a specific energy level (zero) is mapped uniquely onto a distinct action (i.e. there is no different action with the same (0) energy penalty). If the most frequent intent (e.g., intent 1 with likelihood 0.67 for N=2) is consistently mapped to this "do nothing" action, and the second intent to *any* non-zero energy action, the energy distributions would be perfectly separable as shown in Figure 4, leading to high ZS coordination without necessarily requiring a generalizable principle for distinguishing other energy levels. This specific scenario might artificially inflate the observed ZS coordination for N=2.

Another alternative explanation that must be addressed is the exposure bias. Zipfian intent distributions mean that rare intents are sampled significantly less frequently during training (e.g., for N=5, intents 4 and 5 have likelihoods of 0.11 and 0.09 respectively, compared to 0.44 for intent 1, Section 5.3). Therefore, the model has less time to optimize its actions for those rare intents. This inherent imbalance in training exposure may explain some of the differences in energy costs between the rare and frequent actions, even without the explicit incentive for an inverse relationship, making it difficult to isolate the effect of the energy regularization.

Lastly, the architecture choice and problem setting formalization also raise certain concerns. Specifically, the sender policy (actor) only receives the current joint configuration (positions and rotations) at time *t*, concatenated with a fixed intent embedding (Section 3). It has no history of previous states or actions. This does not seem to be an appropriate state specification for generating continuous, fluid "gestures." Coupled with the fact that the policy network is described as a "3-layer feed-forward neural network" (Appendix A.3) with no recurrence, this significantly limits the number and complexity of possible gestures that can be generated. A feedforward network taking only the current state as input can only produce a single action based on that instantaneous state. This contradicts the abstract's description of a "multi-step process that produces an entire trajectory of limb motion," as generating a coherent trajectory typically requires internal state or sequential processing to link actions over time. For example, a "pendulum-like" wave of a hand becomes impossible, since when the hand returns to the middle after the first half-swing, the policy would act as if the action just started, lacking the context of the prior motion. This architectural limitation may severely restrict the emergent complexity of the communication protocols.

*** Conclusion ***

The paper addresses a highly relevant problem and proposes a number of interesting ideas. Unfortunately, in my opinion, some of the proposed ideas (studying communication in an embodied setting, Zipfian intent distribution, costly actions), when introduced together, are not interacting well and are hindering result interpretation. The authors' introduction of discrete domain experiments in the rebuttal (Appendix A.4, A.5) is a welcome addition, providing valuable insights into the core mechanism in a controlled environment.

However, as authors mention, using an embodied setting with multidimensional continuous action and observation spaces makes the problem extremely challenging. Therefore, when we see only partial success or no success in learning, it becomes difficult to identify the true source of the problem. That is, it is unclear if we are hitting a fundamental limit of what can be achieved by the combination of the Zipfian intent + costly actions, or whether it is just a limitation of the architectures/algorithms that the authors used, when those algorithms are applied in a challenging multidimensional setting. The architectural choices, such as the simple feedforward network and the lack of state history for trajectory generation, further complicate this interpretation.

The latter concern is exacerbated by some technical questions. In particular, the choice of a feedforward architecture seems extremely limiting, when combined with the state description that the authors used. The state space for the policy (sender) agent only includes the current joint configuration, without the history. This renders a large number of actions impossible (i.e. the "pendulum-like" wave of a hand becomes impossible, since when the hand returns to the middle after the first half-swing, the policy would act as if the action just started). Lastly, the logic behind the main hypothesis is not fully theoretically supported, and the alternative explanations (such as the "do nothing" action for N=2 concepts, or exposure bias from Zipfian sampling) are not sufficiently addressed.

Overall, I believe that the contribution is extremely promising, and I would love to see an expanded version published. I feel very torn about the decision, but at the moment, I believe that the paper is still below the acceptance threshold, although only marginally. While the core idea of leveraging common-knowledge costs and non-uniform intent distributions for ZS communication is compelling and the discrete domain experiments offer good support, the main embodied experiments are not yet robust or clear enough to fully demonstrate its benefits in this complex setting. I am happy to adjust the score up, and I regret that I can not switch it to an "accept" recommendation.

*** Suggestions ***

I believe that the paper can be made much stronger if the authors take a step back and continue to systematically explore the proposed mechanisms in a simplified communication game setting. The discrete domain experiments (Appendix A.4, A.5) are an excellent start. E.g. instead of actually producing an action trajectory, agents could pick among a number of actions with pre-defined effort levels, or they can separately pick the gesture and the amount of effort to allocate to that gesture (i.e. a subtle hand-wave vs flailing one's arms in the air). This would allow to explore the benefits and limitations of the proposed Zipfian intent + Costly actions approach with greater control and clearer interpretation of results. In that case, demonstrating that the approach also works in an embodied setting would be a beautiful cherry on top, perhaps by augmenting the embodied agents with recurrent architectures or a more complete state representation that allows for complex, time-dependent gestures. Alternatively, it is possible to "boost" the embodiment part of the paper, e.g. by pre-training the agents on some other tasks, thus making certain actions more natural, or by incorporating a stronger inductive bias from biomechanics or motor control literature. Currently, it just seems that embodiment itself is not adding additional insights, but complicating the experiments.

Adding noise (mentioned in Section 3 and Appendix A.3) is a very good idea as intuitively, it seems that it is the only reason why the agents don't converge on an array of extremely subtle actions with near-zero effort, which would be indistinguishable for an observer. I believe that its critical importance in regularizing the continuous communication channel was not discussed enough in the main body of the paper and maybe its role in preventing channel collapse is somewhat overlooked.

*** Questions *** 

- "Furthermore, with an increase in task complexity to 10 concepts, the external observer never performs better than this baseline strategy" (Section 5.3). I thought the baseline accuracy (most frequent class for Zipfian distribution) was 0.34 for N=10 intents. However, looking at Table 2d ("Torque Curriculum"), the condition with "Train Input: $\tau$, Test Input: $\Phi(\tau)$" achieves 0.56, which indeed outperforms the 0.34 baseline. This suggests the statement in the text refers specifically to the "no curriculum" setting (Table 2c), where most results are indeed around or below 0.34, or there is a nuance that needs clarification.
- Apart from the unique zero-effort action, why can't the agents converge on minimally distinguishable actions with the same effort level, especially given the high-dimensional continuous action space and the L2 energy penalty? This relates to the degeneracy problem discussed in the "Technical soundness" section.

*** Typos and other minor suggestions ***

- "execution decentralized" (Section 2.1) -> "decentralized execution".
- "Agent reward" (Section 2.1) is verbally described as a "function of state and actions taken", but the formula is written as if that it is a function of observation and actions taken (ri : Ω × A1 · · · AN → R). This discrepancy should be resolved.
- Table 1 is slightly confusing to read due to formatting; it may be better to make it broader so that the phrase "Train Input" does not break into two lines, improving readability.
- Conflicting notation: N is used as the number of agents on page 3 ("N agents"), but later N refers to the number of concepts (e.g., "N = 2 concepts task" in Table 1). While context usually clarifies, consistent notation (e.g., N_agents and N_concepts) would prevent confusion. Also, the population size of 10 agents (Appendix A.3) is quite small to infer a truly generalizable latent variable, especially if no additional randomness or diversity is explicitly added to the agents during training.
- I personally believe that the use of the term "zero-shot" in this setting is not ideal. The observer is trained on a sample of communication protocols from a population and is then tested on a held-out set of agents from that same population. In my view, it's analogous to "generalization performance" or "out-of-sample coordination," not strictly "zero-shot" performance where the observer has seen *no* examples from *any* partner before testing. I understand that the authors are referring to another study (Hu et al., 2020) that did use "zero-shot" in a similar context, so this consideration did not affect my score. However, before the name for this problem setting is thoroughly established, it may be possible to use "out of sample coordination" or some other alternative option that more accurately reflects the experimental setup.

*** Update after rebuttal ***

I have read the rebuttal and I deeply appreciate the detailed response by the authors. I especially appreciate the introduction of a number of experiments on a simple discrete domain (Appendix A.4, A.5) that help to illustrate the main point of the paper regarding the benefits of Zipfian intent distribution combined with energy costs for ZS communication. This addition significantly strengthens the core theoretical argument.

At the same time, I believe that some serious issues still remain unresolved, particularly concerning the main embodied experiments. For example, my main concern remains: I believe that wrapping the problem in the embodied setting is not introducing additional insights *in this specific experimental setup*. I must clarify that I fully agree that studying how embodiment affects cognition/behavior is an extremely important and exciting area of research. But in the present paper, the models, given their limited architectural capabilities (simple feedforward network, no state history for gestures) and the challenges of the continuous action space, have no real chance to benefit from embodiment (since there is no prior / shared embodied experience or inductive biases for motor control). Instead, they seem to have to solve the task *despite* being placed in an embodied setting, which adds layers of complexity without commensurate gains in insight.

The main insight (in my opinion) is the observation that Zipfian intent distribution together with energy costs could be a good basis for zero-shot communication. I think that additional experiments that the authors introduced in the discrete domain help to strengthen this point, although more experiments could still be beneficial (e.g. systematically varying the population size to further stress the generalization aspect), as well as a more thorough theoretical discussion on degeneracy in continuous spaces. At the same time, the limitations of the main "embodied" experiment remain (most importantly, the fact that fairly high accuracy can be achieved in the N=2 case, potentially because of the unique "do nothing" action trajectory, which isn't a generalizable strategy for N>2).

In short, a large part of the paper (on embodiment) contributes relatively little in terms of its impact and the concrete conclusions that can be drawn from it, which necessarily limits the extent to which the main insightful point can be explored and validated. The main point is truly interesting, however, which makes the paper borderline. The ambition of the work is commendable, but the execution in the embodied setting still needs refinement.

Overall, I believe that the paper is extremely promising and I would love to see an expanded version published. I feel very torn about the decision, but at the moment, I believe that the paper is still below the acceptance threshold, although only marginally. I am happy to adjust the score up, and I regret that I can not switch it to an "accept" recommendation.

As a minor aside - the newly introduced Colab Notebook (http://shorturl.at/luHPX) does not fully run and crashes at cell #4 (model loading) when I tried to explore it, so I can not fully explore the newly introduced experiments. That being said, I think that after fixing, this resource can be very useful in the future. This minor issue did not affect my evaluation.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

Table 1 is slightly confusing to read, it may be better to make it broader so that the phrase "Train Input" does not break into two lines.

Conflicting notation: N is used as the number of agents on page 3, but later N refers to the number of concepts. 10 agents - not enough to infer the latent variable, especially if no randomness is added to the agents.

I personally believe that the use of the term "zero-shot" in this setting is not ideal. The observer is trained on a sample of communication protocols and is then tested on a held-out set. 
In my view, it's analogous to "generalization performance", not "zero-shot" performance. I understand that the authors are referring to another study that did use "zero-shot", so this consideration did not affect my score.
Before the name for this problem setting is thoroughly established, it may be possible to use "out of sample coordination" or some other option.

*** Update after rebuttal ***

I have read the rebuttal and I deeply appreciate the detailed response by the authors. I especially appreciate the introduction of a number of experiments on a simple domain that help to illustrate the main point of the paper.

At the same time, I believe that some serious issues still remain unresolved. For example, my main concern remains: I believe that wrapping the problem in the embodied setting is not introducing additional insights. I must clarify that I fully agree that studying how embodiment affects cognition/behavior is an extremely important and exciting area of research. But in the present paper, the models have no chance to benefit from embodiment (since there is no prior / shared embodied experience), but rather have to solve the task despite being placed in an embodied setting.

The main insight (in my opinion) is the observation that Zipfian intent distribution together with energy costs could be a good basis for zero-shot communication. I think that additional experiments that the authors introduced help to strengthen this point, although more experiments could still be beneficial (e.g. systematically varying the population size), as well as a more thorough theoretical discussion. At the same time, the limitations of the main "embodied" experiment remain (most importantly, the fact that fairly high accuracy can be achieved because of the unique "do nothing" action trajectory). 

In short, a large part of the paper (on embodiment) contributes relatively little in terms of its impact and conclusions that can be drawn from it, which necessarily limits the extent to which the main insightful point can be explored. The main point is truly interesting, however, which makes the paper borderline.

Overall, I believe that the paper is extremely promising and I would love to see an expanded version published. I feel very torn about the decision, but at the moment, I believe that the paper is still below the acceptance threshold, although only marginally. I am happy to adjust the score up, and I regret that I can not switch it to an "accept" recommendation.

As a minor aside - the newly introduced Colab Notebook does not fully run and crashes at the cell #4 (model loading), so I can not fully explore the newly introduced experiments. That being said, I think that after fixing, this resource can be very useful in the future. This minor issue did not affect my evaluation.

Rating:
5: Marginally below acceptance threshold

title: Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning with Diff Pruning

reviews.rating: 8: Top 50% of accepted papers, clear accept
reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This paper proposes diff pruning, an alternative paradigm for parameter-efficient transfer learning of pre-trained models. Similar to adapters, diff pruning leaves the body of the pre-trained model unchanged. Rather than inserting additional task-specific parameters into the pre-trained model, diff pruning adds reparameterizes the parameters of the transferred model $\theta_\tau$ by adding a diff vector $\delta_\tau$ to them: $\theta_\tau = \theta_{\text{pretrained}} + \delta_\tau$. Parameter efficiency is achieved by regularizing $\theta_\tau$ to be sparse. The authors achieve this by using a relaxed mask vector to approximate the $L_0$ norm. They also propose a way to control for a specific sparsity rate via projection onto the $L_0$ ball after training and to enforce group sparsity that takes the model's structure into account. The approach is evaluated on the GLUE benchmark where it achieves competitive performance to full fine-tuning a BERT Large model and adapters while being more parameter-efficient than both of them.

Pros:
1. The proposed method is intuitive and the different modelling choices are principled and well motivated.
2. The method achieves strong results. It is competitive with full fine-tuning and more parameter-efficient than adapters, the prevalent approach for parameter-efficient transfer learning.
3. The authors show how to effectively control the sparsity rate and incorporating structure via group sparsity brings further gains.
4. The authors conduct extensive analyses, which pre-empted many of my questions, such as the variation across different sparsity masks, sparsity patterns across different tasks, etc. Overall, the analyses shed additional light on the characteristics and preferences of different tasks in transfer learning.

Cons:
1. The approach is potentially more complicated than the baseline, so it is important that the authors open-source their code.
2. The diff vector is distributed over the entire set of parameters of the model rather than focused in a few layers. This makes it potentially harder to combine the diff vectors from different tasks as can be done with adapters (see e.g. https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.00247) and to compose multiple diff vectors. 

Questions:
1. Does a visualization of diff vectors of different tasks (such as using t-SNE) reveal any interesting patterns?
2. Are there any transfer settings that the addition of task-specific parameters can model but inserting layer-specific transformations via adapters cannot (or vice versa)? Adapters have been used to transfer across modalities such as languages (see e.g. Pfeiffer et al. (2020), https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.00052) and I am wondering whether the same would be possible by adding task-specific parameters.
3. How long does your approach take to converge in comparison to the baselines? How much longer do you need to fine-tune with non-zero masks for magnitude pruning for sparsity control? What is the performance benefit of this further fine-tuning?

Rating:
8: Top 50% of accepted papers, clear accept
reviews.text:
Review:
This paper proposes diff pruning, an alternative paradigm for parameter-efficient transfer learning of pre-trained models. Similar to adapters, diff pruning leaves the body of the pre-trained model unchanged. Rather than inserting additional task-specific parameters into the pre-trained model, diff pruning reparameterizes the parameters of the transferred model $\theta_\tau$ by adding a diff vector $\delta_\tau$ to them: $\theta_\tau = \theta_{\text{pretrained}} + \delta_\tau$. This "diff vector" represents the task-specific modifications made to the shared pretrained model. Parameter efficiency is achieved by regularizing $\delta_\tau$ to be sparse, minimizing the number of non-zero entries. The authors achieve this by using a relaxed mask vector with a Hard-Concrete distribution to approximate the $L_0$ norm, making it amenable to gradient-based optimization. They also propose a way to control for a specific sparsity rate via projection onto the $L_0$ ball after training using magnitude pruning, and to enforce group sparsity that takes the model's structure into account by defining groups based on matrix/bias vectors. The approach is evaluated on the GLUE benchmark using a BERTLARGE model, where it achieves competitive performance to full fine-tuning and adapters while being more parameter-efficient than both of them, typically requiring only 0.5% additional parameters per task.

Pros:
1.  The proposed method is intuitive and the different modelling choices are principled and well motivated. The core idea of learning a sparse "difference" on top of a fixed pretrained model is conceptually clear and directly addresses the parameter inefficiency of full fine-tuning. The reparameterization $\theta_{\tau} = \theta_{\text{pretrained}} + \delta_{\tau}$ provides a solid foundation for this approach, and the use of differentiable L0 regularization with the Hard-Concrete distribution (Louizos et al., 2018) is a principled way to induce sparsity during training, which is crucial for achieving parameter efficiency.
2.  The method achieves strong results. It is competitive with full fine-tuning and more parameter-efficient than adapters, the prevalent approach for parameter-efficient transfer learning. Specifically, Table 1 demonstrates that structured diff pruning can match the performance of a fully fine-tuned BERTLARGE model on the GLUE benchmark while only requiring 0.5% additional parameters per task. This is a significant improvement in parameter efficiency compared to adapters, which required 2.1% or 3.6% additional parameters for similar performance. The results on the SQuAD extractive question answering task (Table 4) further underscore the method's effectiveness, showing that diff pruning can even surpass the F1 score of the full fine-tuning baseline (e.g., 90.8% to 93.2%) with only 1% additional parameters, suggesting a useful regularization effect.
3.  The authors show how to effectively control the sparsity rate and incorporating structure via group sparsity brings further gains. The L0-ball projection with magnitude pruning (Section 3.2) provides fine-grained control over the exact sparsity rate, which is a practical advantage for applications with specific parameter budgets. Figure 1 (left) clearly illustrates that structured diff pruning consistently outperforms non-structured and non-adaptive variants across a range of target sparsity rates (0.1% to 1.0%), highlighting the benefit of the adaptive learning process and structured regularization. Figure 1 (right) further supports this by showing that structured diff pruning leads to a much higher number of entirely pruned groups, indicating its effectiveness in encouraging clustered sparsity.
4.  The authors conduct extensive analyses, which pre-empted many of my questions, such as the variation across different sparsity masks, sparsity patterns across different tasks, etc. Overall, the analyses shed additional light on the characteristics and preferences of different tasks in transfer learning. Figure 2, for instance, provides valuable insights into how different tasks modify distinct parts of the pretrained network, with qualitative similarities observed between related tasks (e.g., QNLI & QQP, MRPC & STS-B), and consistent sparse modification of the embedding layer across tasks. The analysis in Figure 3 of the appendix on the consistency of sparsity patterns across multiple runs of SST-2 further enhances the understanding of the method's behavior. These detailed investigations significantly strengthen the paper by offering a deeper understanding of *why* the method works and how it interacts with different tasks and model architectures.

Cons:
1.  The approach is potentially more complicated than the baseline, so it is important that the authors open-source their code. The combination of reparameterization, differentiable L0-norm approximation using a stretched Hard-Concrete distribution, and a two-stage training process involving magnitude pruning followed by additional fine-tuning with a fixed mask adds a layer of complexity compared to standard fine-tuning or even adapter-based methods. The authors mention in Section 6.1 that diff pruning takes approximately 1.5x to 2x more time per batch during training, indicating a higher computational cost. Given this complexity, providing the implementation code is essential for reproducibility, verification, and broader adoption by the research community.
2.  The diff vector is distributed over the entire set of parameters of the model rather than focused in a few layers. As shown in Figure 2, the modifications (nonzero diff parameters) are spread across many layers of the BERT model, rather than being confined to specific, modular components. This makes it potentially harder to combine the diff vectors from different tasks as can be done with adapters (see e.g. Pfeiffer et al. (2020a) on AdapterFusion) and to compose multiple diff vectors. The lack of distinct, localized modules, unlike adapter layers which are inserted at specific points, could complicate approaches for non-destructive task composition or combining knowledge from multiple tasks, as any overlap in modified parameters might lead to interference.

Questions:
1.  Does a visualization of diff vectors of different tasks (such as using t-SNE) reveal any interesting patterns?
2.  Are there any transfer settings that the addition of task-specific parameters can model but inserting layer-specific transformations via adapters cannot (or vice versa)? Adapters have been used to transfer across modalities such as languages (see e.g. Pfeiffer et al. (2020c), https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.00052) and I am wondering whether the same would be possible by adding task-specific parameters.
3.  How long does your approach take to converge in comparison to the baselines? How much longer do you need to fine-tune with non-zero masks for magnitude pruning for sparsity control? What is the performance benefit of this further fine-tuning? The authors state in Section 6.1 that "diff pruning takes approxiamtely 1.5x to 2x more time per batch, which results in slower training." Regarding the fine-tuning after magnitude pruning, Section 4.3 mentions it is done for "3 or 5 epochs" with a learning rate of 5e-5. Section 5.4 emphasizes that this additional fine-tuning with the fixed mask was "crucial to maintain good performance," and Table 3 shows minimal performance loss after this step, indicating its importance for the overall effectiveness of the method.

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The authors present an interesting approach to learn task-specific models with only a few tunable parameters. They propose learning a diff vector with a sparsity constraint and then pruning the vector using magnitude pruning. They also impose a structured sparsity constraint by introducing a group penalty.

This work is interesting and important with the growing size of the pretrained models. It enables the model to learn a new task with relatively few parameters would be very beneficial. The experiments and analysis conducted by the authors is thorough.

A few questions/thoughts which could improve the paper: 
* It would be good to list the epochs for training for each of the different approaches? Authors mention 2,3,4,5 epochs for training but would good to highlight the cost or savings of fine tuning with their approach.
* In table 1, it would be also helpful to include some information about the finetuning steps/sec with the different approaches. This would help understand the tradeoff of memory vs compute for the proposed approach.
* What’s the intuition for structured pruning performing better than non unstructured? Typically, enforcing some structure should have worse accuracy. If the structure seems to help, then the model should be able to learn structure without the group constraint as well.

Overall, I recommend accepting the paper. As the size of pre-trained models is growing quite rapidly, research that investigates parameter sharing and adapting a pretrained model to a new task with few parameters is essential.

6: Marginally above acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
The authors present an interesting approach to learn task-specific models with only a few tunable parameters. Their proposed "diff pruning" method views finetuning as learning a task-specific "diff" vector ($\delta_{\tau}$) that is added to the fixed, pretrained parameter vector ($\theta_{pretrained}$), effectively reparameterizing $\theta_{task} = \theta_{pretrained} + \delta_{\tau}$. This diff vector is then regularized with a differentiable approximation to the L0-norm penalty to encourage sparsity, and subsequently pruned using magnitude pruning to achieve an exact sparsity rate. They also impose a structured sparsity constraint by introducing a group penalty, which encourages entire groups of parameters (e.g., matrix/bias vectors) to be pruned together.

This work is particularly interesting and important with the growing size of pretrained models, such as BERTBASE (110M parameters) and BERTLARGE (340M parameters), and even larger models like T5, Megatron-LM, and Turing-NLG with billions of parameters. The standard approach of storing full model parameters for each task becomes prohibitively expensive in multi-task, memory-constrained settings, especially for on-device applications. The ability to learn a new task with relatively few additional parameters per task, while matching the performance of fully finetuned models, would be very beneficial and addresses a critical challenge in scaling NLP models. The experiments and analysis conducted by the authors are thorough, demonstrating that diff pruning can match the performance of fully finetuned baselines on the GLUE benchmark while only modifying 0.5% of the pretrained model’s parameters per task. This is a significant improvement in parameter efficiency compared to adapters (e.g., 3.6% for Adapters (8-256) on GLUE, as shown in Table 1). The paper effectively demonstrates the utility of the differentiable L0-norm approximation with the Hard-Concrete distribution (Section 3.1) and the subsequent L0-ball projection via magnitude pruning (Section 3.2), showing that non-adaptive diff pruning performs significantly worse, highlighting the importance of learning the masking vector. The analysis in Section 5, particularly the varying target sparsity rates (Table 2) and the task-specific sparsity patterns across layers (Figure 2), provides valuable insights into the method's behavior. The results on SQuAD (Table 4) further reinforce the method's effectiveness beyond classification tasks, showing comparable or better performance with significantly fewer parameters (e.g., 1% vs 100% for full finetuning), implying a useful regularization effect.

A few questions/thoughts which could improve the paper:
*   It would be good to list the epochs for training for each of the different approaches explicitly, especially for the baselines like 'Full finetuning' and 'Last layer finetuning'. While the authors mention a hyperparameter search over epochs ∈ {2, 3, 4, 5} for diff pruning and specify finetuning epochs for the fixed mask phase (e.g., 3 or 5 epochs depending on the dataset in Section 4.3), clearly stating the final chosen epochs for *each* method (including baselines) would help readers understand the total computational cost or savings of fine-tuning with their approach. This is particularly relevant given the authors' note in Section 6.1 that diff pruning takes approximately 1.5x to 2x more time per batch, suggesting that training efficiency is a practical consideration.
*   In table 1, it would be also helpful to include some information about the finetuning steps/sec or total training time for the different approaches. This would provide a more complete picture of the tradeoff between memory and compute for the proposed approach, especially in the context of the 'Memory Requirements' discussion in Section 6.1, where the authors acknowledge slower training times due to additional optimization of $\alpha_{\tau}$ and $w_{\tau}$. For practical deployment, understanding not just parameter count but also the time cost of adaptation is crucial.
*   What’s the intuition for structured pruning performing better than non-structured? Typically, enforcing some structure (like group sparsity) might be expected to have worse accuracy or at least not consistently better, as it constrains the model's flexibility. If the structure seems to help, then one might expect the model to be able to learn such structure without the explicit group constraint as well, perhaps by naturally forming sparse blocks. The authors note in Section 5.2 and Figure 1 (right) that structured diff pruning leads to more entire groups being pruned, which is an interesting observation. Further discussion on *why* this structural bias leads to improved performance (e.g., better regularization, more stable learning, or better alignment with inherent model redundancies) would strengthen this point.

Overall, I recommend accepting the paper. As the size of pre-trained models is growing quite rapidly, research that investigates parameter sharing and adapting a pretrained model to a new task with few parameters is essential. The proposed diff pruning method is a novel and highly relevant contribution to this critical area of research.

reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This work studies the problem of parameter-efficient transfer learning in the paradigm of pretraining/finetuning. The proposed method, diff pruning, can match the performance of fully finetuned baselines on the GLUE benchmark while only modifying 0.5 of the pretrained model's parameters per task.

I don't get the problem definition of this work. In other words, the authors need to better motivate and justify parameter efficiency. Given that all the parameters of a big model are used in downstream tasks, what are the benefits of only modifying a few parameters? Here are several possibilities.
1. Does it speed up the finetuning process by modifying a few parameters? 
2. Does it speed up inference for downstream tasks?
3. Another possibility is model size reduction, which leads to reduced storage costs. "This approach can become parameter-efficient as the number of tasks increases as it only requires storing the nonzero positions and weights of the diff vector for each task. The cost of storing the shared pretrained model remains constant and is amortized across multiple tasks." Unfortunately, in real-world scenarios, inference of  large models like BERT_base/large is usually conducted on servers or in cloud, where storage is not a big issue while latency (speed) is critical. Inference in edge devices like phones, IoT sensors cares about storage, speed, and power consumption.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
This work studies the problem of parameter-efficient transfer learning in the paradigm of pretraining/finetuning. The proposed method, diff pruning, can match the performance of fully finetuned baselines on the GLUE benchmark while only modifying 0.5% of the pretrained model's parameters per task. The empirical results presented in Table 1, showing that structured diff pruning can achieve comparable performance to full finetuning with only 0.5% additional parameters per task, are indeed impressive in terms of raw parameter count reduction. The paper also demonstrates that this approach outperforms non-adaptive diff pruning and offers a competitive alternative to adapter layers, which typically require more parameters (e.g., 3.6% for Houlsby et al. (2019) as cited in the Introduction).

However, I don't get the problem definition of this work. In other words, the authors need to better motivate and justify parameter efficiency. While the abstract and introduction highlight "multi-task, memory-constrained settings" and the prohibitive expense of "storing the full model parameters for each task," the practical benefits of only modifying a few parameters are not thoroughly explored or quantified. The paper focuses primarily on the *number* of parameters without a comprehensive analysis of the real-world implications of this reduction.

Here are several possibilities for the benefits of parameter efficiency, which the paper touches upon but does not fully resolve:

1.  **Does it speed up the finetuning process by modifying a few parameters?** The manuscript explicitly states the opposite. In Section 6.1, "Memory Requirements," the authors mention that "While training efﬁciency was not a primary concern of this work, diff pruning takes approxiamtely 1.5× to 2× more time per batch, which results in slower training." This indicates that the proposed method actually increases training time, which is a significant drawback if finetuning speed is a desired form of efficiency.
2.  **Does it speed up inference for downstream tasks?** The paper provides no experimental results or detailed discussion regarding inference speed. Even if only a small "diff vector" is applied, the underlying large pretrained model (e.g., BERTLARGE with 340M parameters) still needs to be loaded and executed. Without specialized sparse computation kernels or hardware, it's unclear how merely having a sparse diff vector would translate into significant inference latency improvements. This is a critical aspect, especially as "inference of large models like BERT_base/large is usually conducted on servers or in cloud, where storage is not a big issue while latency (speed) is critical."
3.  **Another possibility is model size reduction, which leads to reduced storage costs.** The authors articulate this as a primary benefit: "This approach can become parameter-efficient as the number of tasks increases as it only requires storing the nonzero positions and weights of the diff vector for each task. The cost of storing the shared pretrained model remains constant and is amortized across multiple tasks." This is a valid point, particularly for a large number of tasks where the cumulative storage of fully finetuned models would be excessive. However, the paper also notes in Section 6.1 that "storing the task-speciﬁc diff vector requires storing a compressed version with both the nonzero positions and weights, which incurs additional storage requirements" compared to a dense diff vector, though it's still smaller than a full model. The motivation mentions "on-device applications" in the Introduction, suggesting scenarios where storage, speed, and power consumption are all crucial. While diff pruning addresses storage for these settings, its impact on speed and power, which are equally vital for edge devices and not addressed, remains ambiguous.

In summary, while the method achieves impressive sparsity in the task-specific parameters, the broader implications and practical advantages of this parameter efficiency are not convincingly demonstrated. The paper acknowledges its narrow focus on "parameter-efficiency" in Section 6.2, but a more robust justification and analysis of *why* this specific form of efficiency is paramount, especially considering the stated drawbacks in training time and the lack of inference speed analysis, would significantly strengthen the work. Without a clearer articulation and empirical validation of these practical benefits, the core motivation for the proposed approach remains somewhat underdeveloped.

reviews.confidence: 4: The reviewer is confident but not absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
This work combines model pruning with transfer learning/multi-task learning in NLP. Instead of finetuning pretrained models on each individual task, the author propose to learn 'residual' parameters with sparse masks for each task independently, hence reducing per-task parameter requirements. The evaluation on GLUE shows some promising results with better efficiency compared to adapter networks. However, I am concerned with the motivation and applicability of the proposed method, such that I am left with the impression that the method may be hard to use in practice.

Pros:
1. To the best of my knowledge, combining pruning on pretrained models to transfer learning in NLP is novel.
2. Empirical results show the method can performance comparably with adapter networks but with fewer parameters.

Cons (see below for detailed questions):
1. Although the method is proposed for 'multi-task' learning, it is not really evaluated on multi-task settings. The motivation and applicability are not very clear to me.
2. Compared to adapter networks, the proposed method contains more hyper-parameters and is harder to tune in practice.

Questions:
1. In section 2, the author introduced multi-task learning as the background. However, the proposed method is not really designed for multi-task learning (training multiple tasks simultaneously), but rather for transfer learning on multiple tasks independently. In practice, however, training some tasks together can improve performance (GLUE for example), but the proposed method is trained for each task independently and thus there is no positive transfer between tasks. So have you evaluated your method when trained multiple tasks simultaneously (where the shared parameters are jointly finetuned)? How does that work? This is an important extension that might be useful for the community.
2. Please point it out if I miss this information, but how did you select your hyper-parameters for your approach? Besides, how sensitive they are compared to adapter networks?
3. Prior work [1] has shown the resulting mask of pruning can be compared to evaluate task similarities. Did you have similar observations in your experiments?

Missing reference:
The idea of adding 'residual' parameters for new tasks is not new in lifelong learning. It is good to mention [2] and [3]. [1] is also related of comparing pruning masks for pretrained models.

Typo:
Page 4, last line: QQC -> QQP

[1] On negative interference in multilingual models: findings and a meta-learning treatment. Wang et al., EMNLP 2020.

[2] Progressive neural networks. Rusu et al., arxiv 2016

[3] BatchEnsemble: an alternative approach to efficient ensemble and lifelong learning. Wen et al., ICLR 2020.

Review:
This work combines model pruning with transfer learning/multi-task learning in NLP. Instead of finetuning pretrained models on each individual task, the author propose to learn 'residual' parameters with sparse masks for each task independently, hence reducing per-task parameter requirements. The core idea, termed "diff pruning" in Section 3, involves reparameterizing task-specific model parameters as $\theta_{\text{task}} = \theta_{\text{pretrained}} + \delta_{\text{task}}$, where the pretrained model $\theta_{\text{pretrained}}$ remains fixed and shared, and only the task-specific difference vector $\delta_{\text{task}}$ is learned. This $\delta_{\text{task}}$ is encouraged to be sparse via a differentiable approximation to the L0-norm penalty, as detailed in Section 3.1. The evaluation on GLUE shows some promising results with better efficiency compared to adapter networks. However, I am concerned with the motivation and applicability of the proposed method, such that I am left with the impression that the method may be hard to use in practice.

Pros:
1.  To the best of my knowledge, combining pruning on pretrained models to transfer learning in NLP is novel. In the current landscape of increasingly large language models, where storing full copies for each downstream task becomes prohibitively expensive, this approach offers a fresh perspective on how to adapt these powerful models efficiently. The paper's focus on learning a sparse 'diff' vector rather than pruning the entire model for each task is a distinct contribution to parameter-efficient transfer learning, addressing the practical challenges outlined in the Introduction regarding memory-constrained settings.
2.  Empirical results show the method can performance comparably with adapter networks but with fewer parameters. As presented in Table 1, the "Diff pruning (struct.)" method achieves an average GLUE score of 80.6 (excluding QNLI for comparability), which is on par with "Adapters (8-256)" at 80.4 and "Adapters (64)" at 79.8. Critically, this comparable performance is achieved by modifying only 0.5% of the pretrained model's parameters per task, significantly less than the 3.6% and 2.1% required by the respective adapter baselines. This demonstrates a clear advantage in terms of parameter efficiency, aligning with the claims made in the abstract regarding modifying only a small fraction of parameters.

Cons (see below for detailed questions):
1.  Although the method is proposed for 'multi-task' learning, it is not really evaluated on multi-task settings. The motivation and applicability are not very clear to me. While the paper acknowledges in Section 2 (footnote 1) that its setup differs from classic multi-task learning by assuming a "potentially unknown set of tasks," and emphasizes the ability to amortize the cost of storing pretrained parameters across tasks (Section 3), the proposed method trains each task's $\delta_{\text{task}}$ independently. This means there is no mechanism for positive transfer learning *between* tasks during training, which is a key benefit of traditional multi-task learning where tasks are trained simultaneously to learn shared, generalizable representations. The current evaluation only shows parameter efficiency for *multiple* independently trained tasks, not the synergistic benefits of *joint* multi-task learning.
2.  Compared to adapter networks, the proposed method contains more hyper-parameters and is harder to tune in practice. Section 4.3 details several additional hyperparameters specific to the diff pruning approach, including `l` and `r` for stretching the Hard-Concrete distribution, `λ` for weighting the L0-norm penalty, and specific initialization values for the weight vector `wτ` (to 0) and mask parameter `ατ` (to 5). While the authors state that `l = −1.5`, `r = 1.5`, and `λ = 1.25 × 10−7` "worked well across all tasks," and that these hyperparameters also worked for RoBERTa and XLNet in preliminary experiments, the process of arriving at these values and their robustness to different datasets or architectural changes is not thoroughly explored. Adapter networks, by contrast, typically introduce fewer hyperparameters, primarily concerning the bottleneck size (e.g., 8-256 or 64 in Table 1), which can make them simpler to deploy and tune for new applications.

Questions:
1.  In section 2, the author introduced multi-task learning as the background. However, the proposed method is not really designed for multi-task learning (training multiple tasks simultaneously), but rather for transfer learning on multiple tasks independently. In practice, however, training some tasks together can improve performance (GLUE for example), but the proposed method is trained for each task independently and thus there is no positive transfer between tasks. So have you evaluated your method when trained multiple tasks simultaneously (where the shared parameters are jointly finetuned)? How does that work? This is an important extension that might be useful for the community. The current approach, as described in Section 3, focuses on independent learning of $\delta_{\text{task}}$ for each task, which, while parameter-efficient, explicitly foregoes the potential for performance gains through shared learning that joint multi-task training offers. Given that the GLUE benchmark itself is often used in a multi-task setting to demonstrate improved generalization, an evaluation of diff pruning in such a scenario would be highly valuable. Could the diff vectors be learned jointly, or could the shared $\theta_{\text{pretrained}}$ be minimally finetuned in a multi-task fashion?
2.  Please point it out if I miss this information, but how did you select your hyper-parameters for your approach? Besides, how sensitive they are compared to adapter networks? Section 4.3 lists the chosen values for `l`, `r`, `λ`, and initializations for `wτ` and `ατ`. However, the paper only states that these values were "found to work well," without detailing the hyperparameter search strategy (e.g., grid search, random search, specific validation set performance metrics) used to determine them. Understanding the methodology behind selecting these parameters is crucial for reproducibility and for assessing the practical effort involved in applying diff pruning to new models or tasks. Furthermore, Section 5.1 and Table 2 demonstrate that performance is sensitive to the "target sparsity rates," which is directly controlled by `λ`. This implies that `λ` might require careful, task-specific tuning, potentially adding complexity compared to adapter networks where the bottleneck size is often a more intuitive hyperparameter. A more thorough sensitivity analysis across these parameters, especially `λ`, would strengthen the paper.
3.  Prior work [1] has shown the resulting mask of pruning can be compared to evaluate task similarities. Did you have similar observations in your experiments? The paper does explore task-specific sparsity in Section 5.3, with Figure 2 illustrating "the percentage of modified parameters attributable to each layer for different tasks." The authors observe "qualitative similarities between some tasks, for example between QNLI & QQP... and MRPC & STS-B," suggesting that the learned sparse diff vectors indeed capture task-specific modification patterns. Appendix Figure 3 further shows some consistency in sparsity distributions across multiple runs for SST-2. This aligns well with the idea of using pruning masks (or in this case, the distribution of nonzero diff parameters) as a proxy for understanding task relationships or how different tasks recruit different parts of a pretrained model. Further discussion or explicit quantification of these similarities using the masks, potentially referencing the methodology in [1], would enhance this analysis.

Missing reference:
The idea of adding 'residual' parameters for new tasks is not new in lifelong learning. It is good to mention [2] (Progressive Neural Networks by Rusu et al., 2016) and [3] (BatchEnsemble by Wen et al., 2020). These works explore similar concepts of adapting models to new tasks by adding task-specific components or learning efficient ensembles, which could provide additional context to the "diff vector" formulation in Section 3. The concept of using "difference vectors" or additive parameters has roots in these and other continual/lifelong learning approaches aiming to enable adaptation without modifying core shared components. [1] (Wang et al., EMNLP 2020) is also related to comparing pruning masks for pretrained models, which is pertinent to the analysis presented in Section 5.3 and Figure 2.

title: Sparse Linear Networks with a Fixed Butterfly Structure: Theory and Practice

reviews.confidence: 5: The reviewer is absolutely certain that the evaluation is correct and very familiar with the relevant literature
reviews.full_review_text:
Review:
The paper provides an interesting and novel use of butterfly factoziations in encoder-decoder networks. Specifically, the paper proposes replacing the 
encoder with a truncated butterfly network followed by a dense linear layer. The parameters are chosen so as to keep the number of weights in the (replaced) encoder near linear in the input dimension. The authors provide a theoretical result related to auto-encoder optimization.

######

I vote for accepting the paper. The main reason is for my vote is that the main idea introduced is novel and the algorithmic contribution is substantial. 

######

pros 
+ The proposed truncated butterfly network is novel. Aside from the algorithmic contribution, the theorem in the paper raise important questions about
the optimization landscape of butterfly networks. 
+ The paper is clearly written and well justified 
+ Exhaustive literature survey and background on relevant work in both the matrix factorization and neural networks front

######

cons 
- The beginning of section 7 needs to be expanded with more details on the original Indyk et al 2019 paper -- the authors mention that they use the same setting as 
the 2019 paper but the details on how Indyk et al train their network are lacking without looking up the original paper. 

- I think that low matrix approximation experimentation (Sec 7) can be more thorough. Why are only three datasets (in Table 3) used? Additionally and more importantly, Table 4 shows the approximation results for low values of k (max of 30) -- what happens when k=(min(n,d))? Also, the error is measured with respect to the best rank k approximation of X (the eq. following eq. 20) While this way of measuring the error is often used in the low rank matrix approximation literature it is not very informative of the actual approximation quality of \tilde X. It could well be the case that \tilde X = X_k but the error ||X-\tilde X||_F/||X||_F might  be poor hence this way of measuring the approximation performance is a better indicator of how well X is actually approximated in practice.   

######
